2 0 24
NATIONAL DISASTER
RESPONSE PLAN
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

The National Disaster Response Plan (NDRP) 2024 was prepared by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council (NDRRMC), and members of the Response and Early Recovery Pillar led by the Department of
Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) and the Office of Civil Defense (OCD).

The content of the NDRP 2024 draws upon various reviews, consultations, and workshops undertaken by the
OCD and DSWD, with other NDRRMC member agencies, Regional Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Council (RDRRMC) members, Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Councils (LDRRMCs), civil society
organizations (CSOs), and other partners and stakeholders.

The development of the document was supported by the World Bank and the Global Facility for Disaster Reduction
and Recovery (GFDRR). The World Bank team composed of Lesley Cordero (Senior Disaster Risk Management
Specialist), Marilyn Tolosa-Martinez (Senior Disaster Risk Management Specialist), Yasuhiro Kawasoe (DRM
Specialist), Jean De Pano (Urban and DRM Extended Term Consultant), Miguel Dorotan (DRM Consultant), Ma.
Criscia Alonte (Consultant), Ma. Cristina Lee (Consultant), Khristine Hope Porciuncula (Consultant), Bernice Soriano
(Consultant), Christy Faith Balmonte (Consultant), Isabelita Reyes (Consultant), Alexis Laura Feliciano (Consultant),
and Danica Gonzalez (Team Assistant), with guidance from the following government partners:

Office of Civil Defense                         Department of Social Welfare and Development

Asec Hernando M. Caraig Jr                      Asec Irene B. Dumlao, CESO IV, MNSA

The successful updating of the NDRP 2024 was also made possible through the support of the government
agencies, local governments, CSOs and education institutions in the process, especially the following members
of the Technical Working Group: Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD), Office of Civil Defense
(OCD), Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP), Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Foreign Affairs
(DFA), Department of Human Settlements and Urban Development (DHSUD), Department of Information Technology
and Communication (DICT), Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Health (DOH),
Philippine National Police (PNP), Department of Science and Technology – Philippine Institute of Volcanology and
Seismology (DOST-PHIVOLCS), Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical, Astronomical Services Administration (DOST-
PAGASA), Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH), Anti-Terrorism Council-Project Management Center
(ATC-PMC), National Intelligence Coordinating Agency (NICA), Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP), Department of
Agriculture (DA), Department of Environment and Natural Resources – Mines and Geosciences Bureau (DENR-MGB),
Presidential Communications Office (PCO), Philippine Red Cross (PRC), Disaster Risk Reduction Network Philippines
(DRRNetPhils), Ateneo School of Government (ASoG), Philippine Disaster Resilience Foundation (PDRF), and ARISE
Philippines.

x
DEFINITION
OF TERMS
Anticipatory Action       initiatives to be carried out in anticipation of a disaster event based on forecasts
                          and risk assessments: life-saving and life-sustaining actions (one to seven
                          days); meeting essential emergency needs (one to three months); and early
                          recovery actions toward transitioning to long-term recovery (beyond three
                          months).


Civil Society             non-state actors whose aims are neither to generate profits nor to seek
Organizations             governing power. CSOs unite people to advance shared goals and interests.
                          They have a presence in public life, expressing the interests and values of their
                          members or others, and are based on ethical, cultural, scientific, religious or
                          philanthropic considerations. CSOs include nongovernment organizations
                          (NGOs), professional associations, foundations, independent research
                          institutes, community-based organizations (CBOs), faith-based organizations,
                          people's organizations, social movements, and labor unions.


Contingency Planning      a management process that analyzes specific potential events or emerging
                          situations that might threaten society or the environment and establishes
                          arrangements in advance to enable timely, effective, and appropriate responses
                          to such events and situations


Disaster Risk Reduction   the systematic process of using administrative directives, organizations,
and Management            and operational skills and capacities to implement strategies, policies, and
(DRRM)                    improved coping capacities to lessen the adverse impacts of hazards and the
                          possibility of disaster.


Early Recovery            is a multidimensional process of recovery that begins in a humanitarian setting.
                          It is guided by development principles that seek to build on humanitarian
                          programs and catalyze sustainable development opportunities. It aims to
                          generate self-sustaining, nationally-owned, resilient processes for post-crisis
                          recovery. It encompasses the restoration of basic services, livelihoods, shelter,
                          governance, security and rule of law, environment, and social dimensions,
                          including reintegration of displaced populations.



Early Recovery Cluster    leads the assessment for early recovery needs and priorities and establishes
                          mechanisms for the provision of livelihood and other economic opportunities to
                          enable restoration to normal living conditions.


Education Cluster         ensures the safety of learners and personnel and provides continued access to
                          quality education to all affected learners.


Emergency Operations      is a designated facility staffed and equipped with resources to undertake
Center (EOC)              multi-stakeholder coordination, manage information, and facilitate resource
                          mobilization.




                                                                                                                 xi
  Emergency                  provides effective and timely emergency telecommunications services and ICT
  Telecommunications         capacities.
  Cluster (ETC)


  Hazard                     a dangerous phenomenon, substance, human activity, or condition that may
                             cause loss of life, injury, or other health impacts; property damage; loss of
                             livelihood and services; social and economic disruption; and/or environmental
                             damage.


  Hydrometeorological        the atmospheric, hydrological, or oceanographic process or phenomenon that
  Hazards                    may cause loss of life, injury; or other health impacts; property damage; loss of
                             livelihoods and services; social and economic disruption; and/or environmental
                             damage.


  Incident Command           a standardized, on-scene, all-hazard, non-permanent incident management
  System (ICS)               concept activated only in response to disasters and emergencies. ICS
                             allows its users to adopt an integrated organizational structure to match
                             the complexities and demands of single or multiple incidents without being
                             hindered by jurisdictional boundaries.


  Incident Management        a team composed of Command Staff and General Staff who will take the lead
  Team (IMT)                 in ICS implementation.


  Internally Displaced       persons or group of persons forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or
  Persons (IDP) or Persons   places of habitual residence, as a result of or in order to avoid the effects of
  Displaced by a Disaster    natural or human-induced disasters; IDPs have not crossed the internationally
                             recognized State border.


  Internally Displaced       supports and enhances the government’s capacity to ensure protection issues
  Persons (IDP) Protection   do not arise in emergency situations, and to respond and mitigate the effect of
  Cluster                    any protection issues that may arise.


  International              is an external stakeholder that complements the success of the implementation
  Humanitarian               of the National Disaster Response Plan (NDRP). The IHC advances the
  Community (IHC)            collective interests of delivering essential donations and services to support
                             disaster-affected populations and is comprised of responders, donors,
                             and facilitators. The IHC’s engagement is pivotal in fostering a coordinated,
                             international response that aligns with the objectives and strategies outlined in
                             the NDRP.


  Interoperability           the sharing and collaboration of information relevant to disaster preparedness.
                             It requires setting up a common information platform that agencies and
                             institutions can contribute to and can access for informed decision-making in
                             disaster preparedness actions.


  Logistics Cluster          provides an efficient and effective strategic emergency logistics service for all
                             clusters deployed by the National Response Cluster in disaster-affected areas
                             in terms of mobility, warehousing, and supplies and inventory. It has four sub-
                             clusters: Transportation, Warehousing, Supplies and Inventory, and Services.




xii
Management of the        develops the policies, standards, guidelines, systems, and procedures to
Dead and Missing (MDM)   institutionalize MDM among all concerned agencies and stakeholders.
Cluster


Multi-hazard             the selection of multiple major hazards that the country faces; the specific
                         contexts where hazardous events may occur simultaneously or cumulatively
                         over time. The selection takes into account the need to evaluate a hazard’s
                         level of risk given the degrees of exposure and vulnerability in a specific
                         area. It presents the possible impacts on the populace and forms a basis
                         for determining the appropriate level of response actions from national level
                         government agencies down to local government units (LGUs).


Post-Disaster Recovery   the restoration and improvement where appropriate, of facilities, livelihood, and
                         living conditions of disaster-affected communities—including efforts to reduce
                         disaster risk factors—in accordance with the principles of "build back better".


Private Sector           the key actor in the realm of the economy where the central social concern
                         and process are the mutually beneficial production and distribution of goods
                         and services to meet the physical needs of human beings. The private sector
                         comprises private corporations, households and nonprofit institutions serving
                         households.


National Disaster        a working group of various government, non-government, civil sector
Risk Reduction and       organizations, and private sector organizations established under Republic Act
Management Council       10121 and administered by the Office of Civil Defense under the Department of
(NDRRMC)                 National Defense. It is responsible for policy making, coordination, integration,
                         supervision, and monitoring and evaluation. It is also responsible for ensuring
                         the protection and welfare of people during disasters or emergencies.


Rapid Damage             a disaster response mechanism immediately used in the early emergency
Assessment and Needs     phase to determine the extent of impacts and assess the priority needs of
Analysis (RDANA)         affected communities.


Response Clusters        part of the NDRRMC’s strategic action of providing humanitarian assistance
                         and disaster response services. These are organized groups of government
                         agencies designated to undertake coordination functions at the strategic level
                         to provide resource support for tactical response.


Shelter Cluster          deals with the shelter needs of affected communities; including providing
                         resources and capacities for the establishment of temporary shelters for
                         communities rendered homeless.



Terrorism                an act punishable under any of the following provisions of the Revised Penal
                         Code: Article 122; Article 134; Article 134a; Article 248. The crime of terrorism
                         consists of sowing and creating a condition of widespread and extraordinary
                         fear and panic among the populace to coerce the government to give in to an
                         unlawful demand.

                                                                                          xvii
                             REPUBLIC OF THE PHILIPPINES
                       DEPARTMENT OF NATIONAL DEFENSE
               Camp General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City, Metropolitan Manila



        Message of the Secretary of National Defense

                                    Congratulations on the publication of the National Disaster Response Plan 2024
                                    Edition!

                                    The national disaster response plan holds paramount significance as a guiding
                                    document in our efforts to address large-scale hazards affecting the Filipino populace.
                                    While an existing plan is in place, the dynamic nature of these disasters, compounded
                                    by escalating risks posed by climate change, necessitates revisions of our plan. Such
                                    revisions are imperative to ensure our preparedness to effectively assist the Filipino
                                    people during times of emergency.

                                    This plan that will put us together on the same page, preventing confusion and
                                    ensuring unified action, was developed aimed at enhancing our nation’s ability to
                                    respond effectively to disasters. These outlines updated scope, context, triggers,
                                    timelines, and improved tools to facilitate a more synchronized and effective response
                                    from us and our stakeholders.

                                    The NDRP 2024 consolidates three separate plans into a single document for easier
                                    access and reference. This lays the foundation for addressing all hazards while also
                                    including specific provisions for hydrometeorological hazards, earthquakes, tsunamis,
                                    and terrorism-related incidents.
        ATTY. GILBERTO C.
                                    This document is critical for fulfilling our commitment to disaster risk reduction and
          TEODORO, JR               management, ensuring the safety and security of our fellow citizens.
     Chairperson, NDRRMC and
    Secretary of National Defense   I express my sincere appreciation for the collaborative efforts of all individuals involved
                                    in formulating and refining the plan. The development of a comprehensive plan,
                                    adaptable to the evolving landscape and congruent with the latest frameworks, is
                                    indeed imperative.

                                    Once again, I extend my warmest congratulations to everyone on the successful
                                    publication of NDRP 2024!




xviii
                      REPUBLIC OF THE PHILIPPINES
       DEPARTMENT OF SOCIAL WELFARE AND DEVELOPMENT
      IBP Road, Batasan Pambansa Complex, Constitution Hills, Quezon City



    Message of the Secretary of Social Welfare
                and Development

                                  The Philippines is considered one of the most disaster-prone nations worldwide
                                  due to its high level of exposure and susceptibility to natural calamities. Studies
                                  reveal that the country's significant total land area is prone to various hazards.
                                  Annually, communities are impacted by typhoons, storm surges, and heavy
                                  rainfall events, leading to disruptions in economic activities. These occurrences
                                  disproportionately affect the poor, vulnerable, and marginalized segments of the
                                  population.

                                  Given the escalating frequency and severity of disasters and emergencies, it is
                                  imperative for the Philippine to prioritize the continual enhancement of its disaster
                                  response frameworks. While the risks and hazards maybe inevitable, effective
                                  mitigation is attainable through the proficient implementation of relevant policies,
                                  regulations, mechanisms, and programs contained in the National Disaster
                                  Response Plan (NDRP) 2024. This plan is a collaborative initiative involving
                                  member agencies of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
                                  Council (NDRRMC), including the Department of Social Welfare and Development
                                  (DSWD) and the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD).

                                  The NDRP 2024 aims to cultivate a more resilient and disaster-prepared
                                  Philippines. This aligns with the long-term vision of establishing safer, more
                                  adaptive, and disaster-resilient Filipino communities focused on sustainable
                                  development while embracing a proactive and people-centered approach.

                                  The NDRP 2024 underscores a comprehensive approach to disaster
                                  management, emphasizing the urgent need for the Philippines to bolster its
                                  strategies for responding to natural disasters. It aims to streamline coordination
   REX GATCHALIAN                 among government agencies, the private sector, and civil society organizations to
                                  ensure a unified and efficient disaster response. Further, it delineates the specific
Secretary, Department of Social   roles of key stakeholders in implementing disaster response plans and ensuring
  Welfare and Development         synchronized actions during crises. It also accentuates the significance of fostering
                                  more robust information-sharing mechanisms among various organizations
                                  engage in disaster management.

                                  With this, I encourage everyone, especially the various NDRRMC member
                                  agencies, local government and other stakeholders, to execute the response and
                                  early recovery activities outlined in this ensure timely, effective, and coordinated
                                  implementation of disaster response measures for our affected population.
                                  Additionally, the integration of technology into disaster response efforts, particularly
                                  in improving communication and coordination during emergencies, should
                                  continue to be a focus.

                                  Finally, the DSWD acknowledges and appreciates the invaluable contributions of
                                  all those involved in developing the NDRP 2024. Let this plan serve as a guiding
                                  framework for a more effective humanitarian response and service to the people.




                                                                                                                        xix
                              REPUBLIC OF THE PHILIPPINES
                        DEPARTMENT OF NATIONAL DEFENSE
                Camp General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City, Metropolitan Manila



           Message of the Civil Defense Administrator

                                       The Office of Civil Defense (OCD), as one of the lead agencies in the development of
                                       the National Disaster Response Plan 2024, is pleased with its publication.

                                       This updated plan has been carefully developed to further our commitment to
                                       safeguarding the well-being of the Filipino people. It takes into account the evolving
                                       landscape of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in the country, considering
                                       new directions, frameworks, policies, plans, and guidelines.

                                       The 2023 World Risk Index report identified the Philippines as the most disaster-
                                       prone country due to its susceptibility to natural hazards. To address this vulnerability,
                                       it is imperative to implement tighter coordination, unified response, and relevant
                                       approaches, mechanisms, and protocols across all levels of governance or command.

                                       It is crucial to align this plan with the National DRRM Plan 2020–2030, reflect new
                                       provisions on disaster response, integrate social protection strategies, and include
                                       recommendations for training, dissemination, monitoring, and periodic review of the
                                       plan to address the situation effectively.

                                       The NDRP is designed to enhance the nation’s ability to respond efficiently to
                                       emergencies. All involved stakeholders need to align their efforts, and the NDRP serves
                                       as a tool to facilitate this cohesion.

         USEC ARIEL F.                 The NDRP will not only aid in immediate response measures but can also provide
         NEPOMUCENO                    valuable insights for the enhancement of our future emergency response services
                                       through the lessons that we may learn during its implementation.
      Administrator, Office of Civil
             Defense and               As public servants, it is our duty to continually improve our approach to addressing the
     Executive Director, NDRRMC        increasing risks associated with modern times.

                                       Further, I convey my appreciation to everyone involved in the updating of the NDRP.

                                       Congratulations on the publication of the NDRP 2024!

SUMMARY

The Philippines is one of the most disaster-prone countries           risk governance, resilience strengthening by building adaptive,
globally in terms of exposure and vulnerability to natural hazards.   anticipatory, responsive, and transformative capacities,
Around 60 percent of the country’s total land area is exposed         coherence, and mutual reinforcement of strategies amongst
to hazards and at least 74 percent of Filipinos are vulnerable to     actors across thematic pillars. The NDRRMP also supports the
multiple hazards. Climate-related events were the most frequent       priorities and commitments under the Sendai Framework for
disasters recorded in the country. Every year, typhoons, storm        Disaster Risk Reduction.
surge, and heavy rain events affect millions of urban communities
and disrupt economic activities. Annual losses from typhoons are      NDRP 2024 builds on the experiences and lessons from previous
estimated to reach 1.2 percent of the GDP and as much as 4.6          disasters in the last decade to significantly improve the disaster
percent of GDP when super typhoons strike.                            response and early recovery mechanisms and strategies in the
                                                                      country. This document describes the key mechanisms, guiding
Driven by the need to reduce impacts of disaster risks, the           principles, goals and strategies, and institutional arrangements
Philippine government enacted the Philippine Disaster Risk            to effectively plan and implement disaster response and early
Reduction and Management (DRRM) Act of 2010 (RA 10121)                recovery even before disaster happens. Moreover, this document
which reformed the way the country deals with disasters. It           presents the disaster financing options, available technologies
provided the legal and institutional foundation for DRRM in the       and risk information to improve disaster response planning and
country, established the National Disaster Risk Reduction and         implementation. NDRP 2024 also highlights the significant roles
Management Council (NDRRMC) and defined the key roles and             of different institutions from government and private organizations
responsibilities of various DRRM stakeholders.                        that comprised the Response and Early Recovery Clusters
                                                                      focused on specific tasks and responsibilities for effective,
The National Disaster Response Plan (NDRP) 2024 is a strategic        efficient, and more systematic disaster response operations. The
plan that is based on the long-term vision of the country to          NDRP 2024 likewise introduces additional clusters on shelter,
build safer, adaptive, and disaster-resilient Filipino communities    debris clearing and civil works, and crisis communication,
towards sustainable development. It is aligned with the Philippine    increasing people’s awareness and communities’ resilience.
DRRM Act of 2010 that adopted a paradigm shift from reactive          Ultimately, the goal of the NDRP 2024 is to preserve lives,
to proactive approach to disaster risk management. It also            promote immediate assistance to affected communities and

2024 National Disaster Response Plan                                                                                                 xxi
PA R T 1


NDRP
FOUNDATION
Part I provides the background and evolution, purpose, and target users of
the NDRP. Discussed are: 1) key policy frameworks that establish the need
to update the NDRP; 2) the guiding principles and objectives which anchor
actions, disaster response mechanisms, and institutional arrangements,
including the Response Clusters and Incident Management Team (IMT) are
anchored; and the measures to operationalize and implement the NDRP.

CHAPTER 1
INTRODUCTION
This chapter describes the NDRP, its applicability, its evolution,
target users of the plan and the structure of this document.

1   The Geospatial Information Management and Analysis Project for Hazards and Risk Assessment in the Philippines (GeoRiskPH) is a multi-agency
    initiative led by the Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology (PHIVOLCS) funded by the Department of Science and Technology (DOST)
    and monitored by the Philippine Council for Industry, Energy, and Emerging Technology Research and Development (PCIEERD) from 2018–2020.
    It is envisioned to be the Philippines’ central source of information for accurate and efficient hazard and risk assessment to help the government
    increase the nation’s resilience.

2   In 2019, DSWD released a Memorandum Circular No.17 s.2019 (Guidelines in the implementation of the ECT during disasters).

3   PhilSys or the national ID system established under the Philippine Identification System Act or Republic Act 11055, is the government’s main
    identification platform for Filipinos and foreign residents. The Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) has a roadmap on
    “Establishing an Adaptive and Shock Responsive Social Protection System,” with ongoing activities to craft the Philippine Social Protection Plan
    2023-2028. Based on the roadmap, PhilSys can speed up cash transfers if linked to e-payment systems.

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 7
1.3 Target Users
As a strategic plan to guide national-led disaster response, the
target users of the NDRP are the: (1) NDRRMC; (2) member
agencies and organizations of the NDRRMC Response and Early
Recovery Pillar; and (3) NGAs and their regional counterparts;
and the National Response Clusters.4 This NDRP may also serve
as a reference for disaster response plan preparation of LGUs,
NGOs, CSOs, private sector (e.g., business sector, professional
organizations), academe, and volunteer groups, among others.



1.4 Document Structure
The NDRP 2024 is structured as follows:



    Part I                Part I provides the background and evolution, purpose, and target users of the NDRP.
                          It discusses the key policy frameworks that established the need to update the NDRP;
                          the guiding principles and objectives which actions, disaster response mechanisms, and
                          institutional arrangements, including the Response Clusters and Incident Management Team
                          (IMT); are anchored; and the measures to operationalize and implement the NDRP.


    Part II               Parts II to IV are hazard-specific portions, which include the disaster context and scope,
                          triggers for activation and deactivation of NDRP and its operating arms, the disaster response
                          activities at different phases of disaster, and supporting guidelines for events caused by the
                          following:

    Part III               1.   Hydrometeorological Hazards (Part II)

                           2.   Earthquakes and Tsunamis (Part III)
    Part IV
                           3.   Consequence Management of Terrorism-related Incidents (Part IV).




4      The NDRRMC is headed by the DND Secretary as Chairperson with the Secretary of the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG)
       as Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Preparedness, the Secretary of the DSWD as Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Response and Early Recovery, the
       Secretary of the Department of Science and Technology (DOST) as Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Prevention and Mitigation, and the Director-
       General of the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) as Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery. For the other
       members of the National Council, refer to the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 10121.




                                                                                                       8 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
CHAPTER 2
NDRP IN PERSPECTIVE
This chapter provides an overview of Republic Act No. 10121 and the core of the National
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (NDRRMP) 2020–2030 with a particular
emphasis on the National Disaster Response as one of its fundamental thematic pillars. This
highlights the NDRP as an essential element in the strategic management of disasters within
the nation’s Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) framework.




2.1 DRRM Landscape in the Philippines                                           The Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and
                                                                                Management (DRRM) Act
The Philippines ranks first among all countries worldwide in
terms of exposure and vulnerability to disasters caused by                      The Philippine DRRM Act of 2010 (RA 10121) reformed the
natural hazards (World Risk Report, 2023). Around 60 percent                    way the country deals with disasters. It adopted a paradigm
of the country’s total land area and at least 74 percent of                     shift from reactive to proactive approach to disaster risk
Filipinos are vulnerable to multiple hazards (World Bank, 2022).                management. It also provided the legal and institutional
The most catastrophic of these hazards include typhoons,                        foundation for DRRM in the country. It established the National
storm surges, extreme rainfall induced by weather phenomenon                    Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC)

earthquakes. The COVID-19 pandemic further compounded the                       DRRM stakeholders. Following RA 10121, the Government of
impact of disasters by causing job losses, health system stress,                the Philippines (GOP) developed the National Disaster Risk

Disaster events significantly affect the country’s economy.                     Risk Reduction and Management Plan (NDRRMP) 2011–2028.
In 2021, around PHP 60.7 billion (USD 1.1 billion) worth of                     It outlined the goals, outcomes, outputs, and activities under

government spent around PHP 91.9 billion (USD 1.6 billion) per
year, or 0.6 percent of GDP, on disaster response, recovery,
and reconstruction efforts in fiscal year 2015–2018 (Qian et al.,
2020).




5   The Global Terrorism Index is a yearly publication by the Institute for Economics and Peace that assesses and evaluates countries according
    to their levels of terrorism. The assessment of terrorism’s impact is conducted by the analysis of several parameters, including the frequency of
    terrorist occurrences, the number of fatalities, injuries, and the extent of property damage. The index offers vital insights into patterns in worldwide
    terrorism and aids policymakers and security experts in comprehending the changing threat environment.

                                                                                               10 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
Updated NDRRMP
In 2018, the NDRRMP 2011–2018 underwent a comprehensive
review through thematic meetings and consultations that
provided (1) insights into the progress, capacities, policies, and
partnerships, as well as the gaps and challenges faced over
the initial years of its implementation; and (2) opportunities
to integrate lessons generated thus far, and emerging good
practices.

Through a rigorous review, the NDRRMP 2020–2030 was
developed and officially endorsed through the NDRRMC’s
Resolution No. 8 on October 30, 2020. The updated NDRRMP
highlights the updated NDRRMF and serves as a detailed
roadmap for the government and DRRM stakeholders. It is
organized into the following four thematic areas:

•   Disaster Prevention and Mitigation provides strategies,
    activities, and programs designed to avoid the occurrence
    of disasters or to reduce their impact. It involves structural
    and non-structural measures for resilience, risk assessment,
    environmental protection and management, early warning
    systems, among others.

•   Disaster Preparedness centers on ensuring that
    government, communities, households, and all other sectors
    are ready to face an event or a disaster by and enhancing
    their capacity through proper planning such as contingency
    planning, conduct of drills, stockpiling, and others.

•   Disaster Response and Early Recovery focuses on                    targets, strategies, and monitoring mechanisms to reduce
    providing immediate support to affected individuals and            disaster risk, prevent creation of new risk, and increase
    communities when an event or impending emergency                   resilience. This alignment also ensures that disaster response
    or disaster is imminent, ensuring effective coordination           strategies reflect the systemic risks and are coherent across
    of emergency response, and setting the stage for early             various thematic areas and goals. Figure 5 shows the key
    recovery efforts through response plans such as the NDRP           outcomes for each thematic areas to achieve the desired
    and action plans for various hazards.                              resilience vision by 2030.

•   Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery focuses on
                                                                       The new directions identified in the updated NDRRMP include
    restoring and improving the socio-economic conditions of
                                                                       investing in risk-informed activities, digital transformation,
    affected communities, rebuilding infrastructure, and helping
                                                                       multi-sectoral approaches, gender responsiveness, locally
    reduce future disaster risks through the Recovery and
                                                                       led initiatives, inclusive participation, and public-private
    rehabilitation plans.
                                                                       partnerships. In a nutshell, the updated NDRRMP advocates a
                                                                       strategy rooted in resilience, directing how the country prevents
The updated NDRRMP is aligned with the Sendai Framework                and mitigates, prepares for, responds to, and recovers from
for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030,7 which calls for clear          disasters.




7   The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction succeeds Hyogo Framework for Action (HFA) 2005-2015 and complements the Paris
    Agreement on Climate Change, New Urban Agenda, and Sustainable Development Goals.

                  exacerbation of emergency situations through an established and effective disaster response
                  and early recovery operations.

Strategy 1. The integrated platform for information management      Strategy 3. The interoperable disaster response systems
and decision support focuses on creating a comprehensive            emphasize the need to utilize the latest technologies and
system for managing and communicating information before,           establish a collaborative response mechanism that brings
during, and after a disaster. The specific interventions related    together various sectors. This strategy intends to enhance
to this strategy involve establishing early warning systems,        coordination between public and private agencies, including
disseminating crisis information, conducting Pre-Disaster Risk      diverse stakeholders from the private sector entities, CSOs,
Assessments (PDRA), establishing loss and damage reporting          and NGOs that make decisions grounded on science-based
to monitor the response, and conducting post-disaster damage        data and evidence-based information. This strategy seeks to
assessments. This strategy will help build more efficient and       create an efficient and effective response mechanism and to
coordinated disaster response informed by timely, accurate, and     make sure collective expertise and resources are available pre-,
risk-based data.                                                    during, and post-disaster.

Strategy 2. Anticipatory actions help ensure that policy
measures and necessary resources are in place before a
disaster occurs. This includes prepositioning resources,
securing financial support for LGUs in advance, and
implementing adaptive social protection measures. This strategy
will help reduce the time to respond to and recover from disaster
by being prepared and resilient.

CHAPTER 3
DISASTER RESPONSE
MECHANISMS
This chapter explains the disaster response mechanisms adapted by the
NDRRMC, their elements, guiding concepts, processes, and applicable tools.




Disaster response management is organized per disaster phase:        guidance at the national, regional, and local levels. Operating
pre-impact, impact, and post-impact. The applicability of these      procedures of the Response Cluster, IMT, and others are guided
disaster phases varies per hazard type. The Cluster Approach         by protocols at the operational and tactical levels. The concept
and Incident Command System (ICS) are the mechanisms                 of operations for disaster response management also includes
used by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management          mechanisms for requesting international humanitarian assistance
Council (NDRRMC) with the Response Clusters and Incident             (IHA) and coordination with the international humanitarian
Management Team (IMT) in disaster response management                community. The concepts and processes of the disaster
particularly at the operational and tactical level.                  response mechanisms are explained in the succeeding sections.

The Response Cluster and IMT start operating when triggers for
their activation are satisfied. Disaster response operations to be
carried out are defined per disaster phase to provide strategic

System
                                                                          Circular No. 5. s.2007. The approach was institutionalized in
During emergency response, the cluster system aggregates                  the overall Philippine Disaster Management System and was
responders—government agencies, humanitarian organizations,               recognized as a mechanism that can help address the gaps in
faith–based organizations, private sector, and CSOs, including            response and enhance the quality of humanitarian action.9 In
volunteers—according to their specific lines of service or                August 2008, the NDCC used the Response Cluster System
expertise. It defines the roles and responsibilities of each cluster      to address the concerns of IDPs in Mindanao. The clustering
towards a more systematic delivery of responses and services.             of agencies was continually refined in succeeding years as the
The three most important values of the cluster approach system            Response Cluster System was used in various mobilizations.
are: creation of leadership, predictability, and accountability.          Livelihood, for instance, was removed from the Response
Moreover, government agencies are organized in clusters that              Clusters because it was deemed fit to be included in the
work together towards agreed common humanitarian objectives.              Recovery and Rehabilitation Phase.

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 25
Search, Rescue, and Retrieval Cluster
This cluster provides support for an effective, efficient, organized,
and systematic Search, Rescue, and Retrieval operations to
disaster affected areas to minimize casualties and loss of lives. It
coordinates and deploys all available search and rescue teams
from the government, CSOs, private sector, and international
community. The following are the policy guidelines for Search,
Rescue and Retrieval cluster.



             International Search and Rescue Advisory
   >

     approach that aligns relative global best practices on                   NDRRMC Memo No 112 s 2021

     and effective urban search and rescue (USAR)                             Guidelines on the Philippines Urban

     countries affected by sudden-onset disasters, most                       Accreditation Process

     as structural collapse. The INSARAG also guide
     international USAR teams responding in affected

     countries.

                                                                        Guidelines. It covers accreditation of USAR teams in
     The INSARAG framework emphasizes interoperability
                                                                        the Philippines.
     across different levels of USAR and extends to local,
     regional, and international agencies and teams.
                                                                        The issuance defines key terms and establishes a
     The framework recognizes that common working

                                                                                      26 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
Health Cluster
The Health Cluster ensures that affected populations have
access to appropriate, high quality, and timely health services.
The cluster’s objective is to save lives and decrease mortalities
and preventable mortalities, morbidities, injuries, and disabilities.
Health has four subclusters: Public Health and Medical; Water,
Sanitation, and Hygiene (WASH); Nutrition; and Mental Health
and Psychosocial Support (MHPSS).

Camp Coordination and Camp Management
(CCCM) Cluster
The CCCM Cluster provides timely and well-coordinated
humanitarian assistance and augmentation support in managing
evacuation centers. Below is the policy guideline for the CCCM          IDP Protection Cluster
cluster.
                                                                        The IDP Protection Cluster supports and enhances the
                                                                        government’s capacity to ensure protection issues do not arise in
             Republic Act 10821                                         emergency situations and to respond to and mitigate the effect of
                                                                        any protection issues that do arise.

                                                                  >
                                                                         during Disaster


                                                                  DSWD also facilitates the implementation of the
                                                                  Emergency Cash Transfer Program during disasters.
                                                                  The objective of this approach is to expedite
                                                                  assistance and to provide disaster victims with a
                                                                  sense of dignity and autonomy in determining their
                                                                  immediate needs.

                                                                  The MC 017, s.2019, “Guidelines in the
                                                                  Implementation of the ECT Program during Disaster,”
                                                                  focuses on unconditional cash assistance to families
Food and Non-Food Items (FNI) Cluster                             affected by disasters in the Philippines and aims to
                                                                  ensure prompt, efficient, and effective delivery of ECT
The Food and Non-Food Items Cluster provides nutritious, age-     while maintaining transparency and accountability
appropriate, and socially and culturally acceptable food, along   among implementing agencies.
with essential non-food items to the affected population during
the emergency response phase, and seeks to restore at least the   It outlines policies and guidelines for the entire
pre-disaster level of food security in the long-term.             ECT implementation cycle, including beneficiary
                                                                  identification, validation and registration,
                                                                  determination of transfer amounts, selection of
            Memorandum Circular No. 24, Series                    disbursement mechanisms, payout processes,
            of 2024                                               and the subsequent monitoring and evaluation of
                                                                  ECT operations. The standards and procedures
            Enhanced Omnibus Guidelines on Food                   provided include the use of the Disaster Assistance
            and Non-Food Items and Logistics                      Family Access Card, now called the Family Access
            Management for Disaster Response                      Card in Emergencies and Disasters or FACED,
            Operations
    >




                                                                  documentation and reporting requirements, a
                                                                  grievance redress system, and coordination and
                                                                  communication mechanisms.
     This MC provides operational clarity on managing and
     mobilizing Food and Non-Food Items (FNI) and funding
     to ensure the effective, efficient, and coordinated
     delivery of immediate relief and early recovery services
     to affected populations.

     The MC defines the roles and responsibilities of the
     Department of Social Welfare and Development
     (DSWD) central, field, and attached offices, as
     well as partner agencies and stakeholders, in the
     management of FNIs.

     Additionally, this guideline encompasses the entire
     FNI management cycle, from needs assessment
     to reporting and evaluation, while incorporating
     standards and specifications for various FNIs to ensure
     consistent and quality service delivery.




                                                                                  28 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
Logistics Cluster
The Logistics Cluster provides an efficient and effective strategic
emergency logistics service for all clusters deployed by the
National Response Clusters in disaster affected areas. It has
four sub clusters: Transportation, Warehousing, Supplies and
Inventory, and Services or Infrastructure and Utilities




                                                                      Education Cluster
                                                                      The Education Cluster ensures the safety of learners and
                                                                      teaching and non-teaching personnel. It also provides affected
                                                                      learners with continued access to quality education. Below is the
                                                                      policy guideline for the education cluster.

3.1.2 Incident Command System (ICS)
The ICS is a standard, on-scene, all-hazard incident
management concept that can be used by DRRMCs at all levels,
particularly response groups. The purpose of the ICS is to help
ensure the safety of responders and the public, achievement of
tactical objectives, and efficient use of resources.

The system meets the needs of incidents of any kind or size. It
allows personnel from a variety of agencies to meld rapidly into
one management structure that uses a common or the same
language. It maximizes effective logistical and administrative
support to operational staff by establishing accountability and a
planning process to avoid duplication of efforts.




                                                                                         34 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
   Legal Basis of the Incident Command System


   RA No. 10121                   •     Section 9 (g): The Office of Civil Defense (OCD) shall formulate standard operating
                                        procedures for coordination.

                                  •     Rule 7 (h), Implementing Rules and Regulations: The OCD shall establish ICS as part
                                        of the Philippine DRRM System.

   s2012                          Philippine DRRM System.

   s2012                          crises.

   Order No. 43, s2016            Response Clusters.


   NDRRMC Memorandum              Signed on August 18, 2016, this provides guidelines on the mobilization of IMTs.
   Order No. 44, s2016

The assumption of authority (escalation protocol) follows Section
15 (Coordination During Emergencies) of RA No. 10121 in
preparing for, responding to, and recovering from the impacts of
disasters based on the following criteria:

a. Barangay Development Committee if a barangay is affected;
b. Municipal/City DRRMC, if two or more barangays are
   affected;
c. Provincial DRRMC, if two or more municipalities/cities are
   affected;
d. Regional DRRMC, if two or more provinces are affected; and
e. NDRRMC, if two or more regions are affected.

The NDRRMC and intermediary local DRRMCs shall always act
                                                                       FIGURE 15. Response and Early Recovery Pillar Orientation on
as support to LGUs which have the primary responsibility as first
                                                                       Escalation Protocol
disaster responders. Private sector groups, CSOs, and other
volunteer groups shall work based on coordination mechanisms
and policies set by the NDRRMC and concerned local DRRMCs.                          ESCALATION PROTOCOL
This is in line with the provisions of RA 10121 acknowledging the
jurisdiction of the different levels of the local government and the

3.2.5. Early Recovery
                                                                            As stated in the NDRRMP 2020-2030, the following
Early Recovery is a multidimensional process of recovery that               are the identified Early Recovery indicators:
begins in a humanitarian setting. It is guided by development
principles that seek to build on humanitarian programs and                  1.   number of persons with continuing assistance
catalyze sustainable development opportunities. It aims to                       (financial and livelihood).
generate self-sustaining, nationally owned, resilient processes
                                                                            2.   number of vulnerable populations provided with
for post-crisis recovery. It encompasses the restoration of basic
                                                                                 adequate and appropriate risk protection measures.
services, livelihoods, shelter, governance, security and rule of law,
environment, and social dimensions, including reintegration of              3.   essential health services based on standards; and
displaced populations. Such actions are manifested when there               4.   operationalized early recovery programs.
is a decrease in evacuees and search and rescue assistance.
Normalcy is felt when markets and critical economic facilities
such as banks, stores, and gas stations, transport systems,
hospitals, communications, etc. start to function.                      3.2.6. Disaster Information Management Tools
                                                                        and Systems
Guided by the NDRRMC Memorandum No. 80, s.2022 (Disaster

3.3 Demobilization and Deactivation                                 and demobilized.

The demobilization and deactivation of Response Clusters and
IMT at the national level may start with observation of the Early
Recovery phase. Individual response clusters may be deactivated
and demobilized separately depending on existing situation and
specific needs on the ground.




                                                                                          46 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
CHAPTER 4
OPERATIONALIZING
THE NDRP
This chapter provides guidance on how to operationalize the NDRP. It
describes the interoperability of response clusters and IMT, and the set-up
of EOC. It also discusses the engagement of other stakeholders such as the
military, IHC and private sector; disaster response financing options, training
and dissemination activities, and implementation of the NDRP.




4.1 Interoperability of Response Clusters                           The Response Clusters act as resource provider and the
                                                                    IMT acts as resource employer and is linked by the DRRM–
and IMT                                                             Emergency Operations Center (EOC). The Responsible Officer
                                                                    and principals from other agencies undertake coordination by:
Most disasters and emergencies are managed by the Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Councils (DRRMC) at different         •   making policy decisions;
levels depending on severity and magnitude. The DRRMCs use
the tenets of the Incident Command System (ICS) and Cluster         •   establishing priorities;
Approach System (CAS), which provide the structure and              •   resolving critical resource issues;
operational facility to coordinate and respond with coherence and
                                                                    •   mobilizing and tracking resources; and
flexibility, following the concept of "Resource Provider-Response
Clusters" and "Resource Employer-Incident Management Team".         •   collecting, analyzing, and disseminating situation reports/
The NDRP can be implemented through the simultaneous                    information.
activation of these systems.

The NDRRMC Memorandum No. 78, s. 2021 "Emergency Alert
and Warning Messages Standard Operating Procedures" outlines
the procedures for sending emergency alerts and warnings to
the public in case of disasters. The memorandum was signed in
August 2021 and implemented in compliance with Republic Act
10639 or the Free Mobile Disaster Alerts Law, which mandates
the provision of free mobile alerts during calamities.

                                                                                              52 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
4.6 Private Sector Engagement
Government by itself cannot address all needs arising from a
disaster. This is why the system to respond to a major event
requires a whole-of-society approach where all stakeholders are
activated, engaged, and integrated. The approach maximizes
available resources across sectors including the private sector
which is comprised of private corporations, households, and
nonprofit institutions and plays a critical role in providing support
services and coordinating humanitarian assistance in times of
emergencies. Companies that own and operate utilities are at the
forefront of reestablishing and stabilizing lifelines. Business can
also provide support in the areas of supply chains for essential
needs.

Private sector groups and alliances directly coordinate response
and early recovery efforts with specific clusters. For instance,
for the provision of health assistance, private organizations
coordinate with the DOH. For the provision of food and NFIs, the
private sector is usually referred to the OCD and DSWD.                 Example of Public-Private Partnership Arrangement

The Government is also encouraged to leverage partnerships              Governments and disaster management agencies in
with the private sector by establishing partnership framework           other countries like Japan, Indonesia and USA usually
agreements to efficiently mobilize resources, technical expertise       enter into framework contracts and pre-negotiated
and ensure business operations in disaster-affected areas.              agreements for goods and services including civil works,
Framework agreements for procurement of emergency works,                that would be needed for post-disaster. Civil works such
goods, and non-consulting services, and consulting service              as repairs and reconstruction of roads, bridges and other
can be arranged by the government even before a disaster or             critical lifeline infrastructure including relief goods and food
emergency happens. Framework agreement is a long-term                   packs, construction materials and shelter kits are some
agreement with suppliers, contractors and providers of non-             of the goods and services that can be included under this
consulting services that explains the terms and conditions              partnership arrangements. The Philippine procurement
under which specific procurements can be executed. These                law and its rules also allow similar arrangements. Also
are generally based on prices that are either pre-agreed or             called as "ordering agreements", the framework contracts
determined at the procurement stage through competition or a            can be utilized by the national and local governments
process allowing their revision without further competition. Prior      to speed up the procurement process specifically for
arrangements allow the offering and signing of contracts, whose         disaster emergency and response.
execution is only triggered when a disaster strikes. Below is an
example of possible public-private partnerships arrangements            Source: Philippines: Lessons Learned from Typhoon
that can be utilized for disaster response.                             Yolanda. Policy Notes. October 2017.

structured feedback mechanism to assess the performance                  Response and Early Recovery Pillar will formulate a Monitoring
and progress concerning the targets outlined in the plan. These          and Evaluation Framework that aligns with the principles of the

Within the Response and Early Recovery Pillar, there will be a           focus on the indicators outlines in the SFDRR 2015–2030 and

in relation to targeted outcomes. These reviews aim to identify
gaps and bottlenecks, and prescribe necessary actions aligned
with the long-term goals of the NDRRMP. The reviews will be
conducted in accordance with the Results-Based Monitoring and
Evaluation System (RBMES) of the NDRRMP.




                                                                                               56 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
PA R T 2


HYDRO-
METEOROLOGICAL
HAZARDS
Most of the disaster events in the country are hydrometeorological in nature. Tropical
cyclones, specifically typhoons, are the most common hydrometeorological hazard in the
Philippines. Rare catastrophic events, such as Super Typhoon Haiyan, resulted in enormous
economic losses. Part 2 discusses the Philippines' response plan for hydrometeorological
hazards, specifically typhoons.




57–75
2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 57
58 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
CHAPTER 1
CONTEXT & SCOPE


Hydrometeorological hazards is defined as "the process or              Tropical cyclones, specifically typhoons, are the most common
phenomenon of atmospheric, hydrological, or oceanographic              hydrometeorological hazard in the Philippines. Typhoons often
nature that may cause loss of life, injury or other health impacts,    lead to flooding, storm surges, rain-induced landslides, and other
property damage, loss of livelihoods and services, social and          secondary effects such as damages to infrastructure, including
economic disruption, or environmental damage" (UNISDR).                dams and water reservoirs. The DOST-PAGASA is the technical

not limited to tropical cyclones (typhoons and hurricanes),            cyclones.
extreme rainfall induced by weather phenomenon (including
shear line), floods (including flash floods), coastal storm surges,    The Philippines lies in the Pacific Typhoon Belt and is exposed
thunderstorms, heatwaves, and drought. Hydrometeorological             to more than 20 typhoons each year, five of which are usually
hazards can also entail other risks like wildfires, landslides, and    stronger typhoons.13 The overview of the tracks of the tropical

The disaster response operation for Typhoon Odette is an               is also a sample case where a special team was organized to
example where early response actions, such as raising the              address a more complex emergency situation. Background on
alert status, activating clusters, and following Emergency             Typhoon Odette and the timeline of events and disaster response
Preparedness and Response (EPR) protocols, were done prior             activities are in the box below.
to the issuance of tropical cyclone wind signal (TCWS) no.2. It




           Response Operations for Typhoon Odette (Rai)

           From December 14 to 18, 2021, Typhoon Odette raged within the PAR. Odette’s strength of 195 kph and
           gustiness of 270 kph rated it tropical cyclone wind signal (TCWS) no. 4—the highest wind signal. Typhoon
           Odette affected 2.27 million families (7.85 million persons), and 11 out of 17 regions in the country. The regions
           hit the hardest were MIMAROPA, Region VI, VII, VIII, and CARAGA. Resulting damage was at almost PhP 48
           billion: PhP 62.68 million were damages to houses, PhP 29.76 billion to infrastructure, and PhP 17.75 billion
           to agriculture. (Typhoon Odette was second to Super Typhoon Yolanda in terms of affected individuals and
           damage.)


            Timeline           Disaster Response Activities

            10 Dec. 2021       NDRRMC conducted first weather updating session with all RDRRMCs

            12 Dec.2021         •    STS RAI was detected as an LPA.
                                •    NDRRMC conducted a weather updating session.

            13 Dec. 2021       PDRA was initiated by NDRRMC.

            14 Dec. 2021        •    Severe Tropical Storm Rai entered PAR and named Typhoon Odette.
                                •    NDRRMC EOC Management Team and virtual EOC were activated.
                                •    CHARLIE protocol was activated. RED Alert Status was raised.
                                •    NDRRMC Memorandum No. 147, s.2021 was issued to activate eight clusters
                                     (Logistics, ETC, FNI, CCCM, IDP Protection, Health, Education, and SRR) and put
                                     3 clusters on standby (LAO, MDM, and PIHA).
                                •    NDRRMC Memorandum No. 148, s.2021 was issued to activate EPR protocols.

            15 Dec. 2021        •    TCWS No. 2 was issued over eastern portion of Surigao del Norte and Surigao del
                                     Sur; TCWS No. 3 (11:00 p.m.) over Dinagat Islands, Surigao del Norte, Agusan del
                                     Norte and Surigao del Sur.
                                •    Weather updating sessions and a series of virtual EOC briefings were held.

            16 Dec. 2021        •    TCWS No. 4 over S. Leyte, Bohol, Dinagat Islands, Surigao del Norte. Seven
                                     landfalls were recorded.

In a Hydrometeorological setting, the three phases of disasters   Since hydrometeorological hazards are slow onset events,
are applicable and defined as follows:                            responding agencies and organizations have time to prepare
                                                                  before the disturbance intensifies into a disaster event. Aside
 Pre-Impact        before entry of tropical cyclone to the PAR    from this, it is anticipated that DRRMCs have put in place
 Phase             or its formation within the PAR                disaster response mechanisms due to the frequent occurrence of
                                                                  typhoons and based on past experiences with having to respond
 Impact            PAR or its formation within the PAR            to hydrometeorological events. The succeeding sections discuss
 Phase                                                            the actions and processes per disaster phase including important
                                                                  activities, responsible actors, and relevant guidelines.
 Post-Impact       period where the National Disaster Risk
 Phase             Reduction and Management Council
                   (DRRMC) through the Vice-Chair for
                   response (DSWD) will declare the end/
                   waning strong winds and rain, and tropical
                   cyclone has exited PAR.

 1      Raise Blue or Red Alert Status at the Operations Center.                      DRRMCs, OCD

        the National or Local levels, as necessary, and mobilize multi-stakeholder,

        and implement heightened safety and security measures.

        hospitals, among others.

 7      Develop and release Disaster Situational Report.                              DRRMCs, OCD

        Analysis (RDANA).

        Humanitarian Country Team to determine the immediate relief and
        response requirements.

        Incident Command System (ICS).

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 75
PA R T 3


EARTHQUAKES
& TSUNAMIS
The Philippines is located in the Pacific Ring of Fire where most seismic
events concentrate, such as earthquakes and tsunamis generated
by tectonic processes. Part 3 covers Philippines’ response plan for
earthquakes, tsunamis, and their related hazards. This part also discusses
the associated risks such as fire events, exposure to hazardous materials,
and damage to critical infrastructure, among others. Hazards and risks
related to volcanic eruption are not covered.




76–98                                                      76 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 77
CHAPTER 1
CONTEXT & SCOPE

Earthquake is defined as a weak to violent shaking of the ground
produced by the sudden movement of rock materials below the
earth’s surface. Hazards generated by earthquakes are ground
rupture, ground shaking, liquefaction, earthquake-induced
landslide, and tsunamis. There are two types of earthquakes – (i)
Tectonic earthquakes are produced by sudden movement along
faults and plate boundaries. (ii) Volcanic earthquakes are induced
by rising lava or magma beneath active volcanoes. Earthquake
strength is measured by magnitude and intensity. Magnitude
is proportional to the energy released by an earthquake at the
focus and is represented by Arabic Numbers (e.g., 4.8, 9.0).
Intensity is the strength of an earthquake as perceived and
felt by people and is represented in Roman Numerals (e.g., II,
IV, IX). The intensity of an earthquake is determined using the
PHIVOLCS Earthquake Intensity Scale (PEIS). As of March
2024, DOST-PHIVOLCS operates and maintains 125 seismic
monitoring stations all over the Philippines. Data is sent to the
PHIVOLCS Data Receiving Center to determine earthquake
magnitude, depth of focus, and epicenter. Together with reported
felt intensities in the area (if any), earthquake information is
released once data is gathered (DOST-PHIVOLCS).

A Tsunami is a series of sea waves that are commonly generated
by under-the-sea earthquakes and that may have heights greater
than five meters. Effects of a tsunami include flooding, coastal
erosion, loss of life, and damage to properties. There are two
types of tsunami generation: Local and Far Field or distant
tsunami. The coastal areas in the Philippines especially those
facing the Pacific Ocean, West Philippine Sea, Sulu Sea, and
Celebes Sea can be affected by tsunamis that may be generated
by local earthquakes. Signs of an impending tsunami include felt
earthquake, unusual sea level change, and rumbling sound of
approaching waves (DOST-PHIVOLCS).

The Philippines is located in the Pacific Ring of Fire which is
associated with a nearly continuous series of oceanic trenches,
volcanic arcs, and volcanic belts and/or plate movements
(Figure 18). The circum-Pacific Ring of Fire, characterized by the
convergence of the Pacific Plate with many adjacent tectonic
plates is where most seismic events concentrate. These include
earthquakes and volcanic eruptions. (U.S. Geological Survey,
2023). Considered a critical area, it serves as one of the primary
border regions for the tectonic plates of over half of the world,
thereby affecting millions of people living in these areas.




                                                                     78 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
FIGURE 18. The Pacific Ring of Fire




Source: Ring of Fire [This Dynamic Earth, USGS], n.d.

In October 2019, a series of earthquakes with epicenter near
Tulunan, North Cotabato affected several regions in Mindanao.               For additional information about
These earthquakes were accompanied by aftershocks and                       earthquake and its related
resulted in damage to infrastructure, death and injuries, and               hazards see:
displacement of affected communities, among others. The
October 2019 series of earthquakes in Mindanao is one of the                • DOST–PHIVOLCS Website which contains
more recent extensive and complex earthquake events that                      publications, IEC materials, and learning
required simultaneous actions of multiple DRRMCs at the local,                tools.
regional, and national levels. The box below presents a timeline
of the event and the government’s experience in handling
earthquake response operations.




               Response Operations for the October 2019 Series of Earthquakes in the
                                      Province of Cotabato

          Magnitude 6.3 earthquake:

          On October 16, 2019, a magnitude 6.3 earthquake occurred at 07:37 p.m. in Tulunan, Cotabato. Ground shaking
          was felt at varying intensities by communities in Regions IX, X, XI, XII, CARAGA, and Bangsamoro Autonomous
          Region is Muslim Mindanao (BARMM). The highest intensity was Intensity VII in portions of the Provinces of
          Cotabato and Davao del Sur. A total of 870 aftershocks were recorded. Around 31,000 families (148,000 persons)
          in Regions XI and XII were affected. Around 8,000 families (36,000 persons) were displaced.

          Magnitude 6.6 and 6.5 earthquakes on October 29 to 31, 2019:

          On October 29, a magnitude 6.6 earthquake occurred in the same vicinity at 09:04 a.m., followed by a magnitude
          6.1 earthquake at 10:42 a.m. Then on October 31, a magnitude 6.5 earthquake occurred at 09:11 a.m.. Ground
          shaking was felt at varying intensities by communities in Regions IX, X, XI, XII, CARAGA, and Bangsamoro
          Autonomous Region is Muslim Mindanao (BARMM). The highest intensity was Intensity VII in portions of the
          Provinces of Cotabato, Davao del Sur, and Sarangani. The earthquake affected an estimate of 72,000 families
          (356,000 persons) in Regions XI and XII and displaced about 18,000 families (86,000 persons).

          The timeline of events and activities conducted are summarized below:

            Timelines            Disaster Events, Response and Activities

            Oct 16, 2019         •   A magnitude 6.3 earthquake occurred in Tulunan, North Cotabato.

                                 •   Emergency Alert Warning Messages (EAWM) were sent by National Disaster Risk
                                     Reduction and Management Council Operations Center (NDRRMOC) to telcos for
                                     dissemination to Tulunan, North Cotabato.

                    Analysis (RDANA) team.

                    Province of Cotabato.
                •   State of Calamity was declared in:
                     - Magsaysay, Davao del Sur in Region XI (Resolution No. 99 s. 2019);
                     - Bansalan, Davao del Sur in Region XI (Resolution No. 378 s. 2019).

                    in Tulunan, Cotabato Province in Region XII (Resolution No. 244 s. 2019).

                    (Resolution No. 19-369).

                    119-2019).

Oct. 29, 2019   •   PHIVOLCS recorded a magnitude 6.6 earthquake in the province of North Cotabato.
                    "No Tsunami Threat" advisory was issued since the earthquake was inland.

                •   OCD XI/RDRRMC XI conducted Emergency Response Clusters’ Meeting (2:00 p.m.).
                •   Provincial DRRMC Davao del Norte issued province-wide work and class suspension.
