                              •   OCD alerted SRR teams for deployment to Davao City on 20 October 2019.
                              •   DSWD Central Office started the delivery of various non-food items.
                              •   Classes were suspended in Regions IX, X, XI, XII, and BARMM; work was suspended
                                  in Regions X, XI, XII, and BARMM.


           Oct. 31, 2019      •   A magnitude 6.5 earthquake rocked central and eastern Mindanao. "No Tsunami
                                  Threat" advisory was issued since the earthquake was inland.
                              •   Water interruption occurred due to damage in the Metro Kidapawan Water District,
                                  Cotabato.
                              •   State of Calamity was declared in Malalag, Davao del Sur in Region XI (Resolution No.
                                  2019-175).
                              •   NDRRMC conducted Response Cluster meetings (3:00 p.m.).
                              •   OCD Regional DRRMC deployed search and rescue units (SRU) and search and
                                  retrieval (SAR) teams.
                              •   Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) XII issued memorandum on
                                  Earthquake Preparedness in SOCCSKSARGEN, and disseminated DILG Advisory on
                                  Precautionary Measures to undertake after an incident.
                              •   Aerial survey was conducted by PHIVOLCS, Mines and Geosciences Bureau (MGB)
                                  XII, Philippine Airforce Group Tactical Operations Group (PAG TOG) II, and Bureau of
                                  Fire Protection (BFP) XII.


           Nov. 01, 2019      •   OCD conducted ocular inspection and situation briefing in OCD Regional Office (RO)
                                  XII.
                              •   DSWD ROX deployed 11 regional Quick Response Team members for augmentation
                                  support to Field Office XI.

           Nov. 02, 2019      •   DILG XII conducted on-site meeting with LCEs of selected LGUs.
                              •   Damaged water pipes in M’lang, Cotabato were repaired; water was restored.

        teams to home units

PA R T 4


CONSEQUENCE
MANAGEMENT
OF TERRORISM-
RELATED
INCIDENTS
Part 4 focuses on providing response and early recovery interventions to affected
populations in the context of emergency response. By serving as a guide for resource
augmentation to support response operations, this part also supplements the National
Crisis Management Core Manual and Practical Guide for National Crisis Managers.




99–116
2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 99
100 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
CHAPTER 1
CONTEXT & SCOPE

Aside from terrorism, human-induced crises also include                 includes determination of the appropriate level of response
threats to maritime borders, overseas Filipino workers,                 based on needs assessment and availability of resources.
national security, public health, the economy, and energy
supply. Terrorism-related incidents that are covered by this            Aside from the NDRRMC and the NCMC, a key strategic
NDRP are acts intended to "intimidate the general public                stakeholder for Terrorism-Related Incidents is the Anti-
or a segment thereof, create an atmosphere or spread of                 terrorism Council (ATC). The ATC assumes overall
a message of fear, to provoke or influence by intimidation              responsibility for the proper and effective implementation
the government or any international organization, or                    of the policies of the State against terrorism. The ATC
seriously destabilize or destroy the fundamental political,             shall formulate and adopt comprehensive, adequate,
economic, or social structures of the country or create a               efficient, and effective plans, programs, and measures to
public emergency or seriously undermine public safety".15               prevent, counter, suppress, and/or eradicate terrorism in
Part IV focuses on providing response and early recovery                the country and to protect the public from such acts. The

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 101
FIGURE 20. National Crisis Management Framework




The concept of "Predict" involves the conduct of horizon scanning for emerging threats and conveying warnings at the strategic,
operational, and tactical levels. "Prevent" involves the preparation of intra- and inter-agency contingency and action plans. "Prepare"
encompasses capability-building activities such as equipage, training, coordination, and information sharing.




                                                                                          102 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
     Marawi Siege (May to October 2017)

     The Marawi Siege was a significant terrorism-related
     incident. The five-month siege damaged Marawi
     City’s infrastructure, including schools, hospitals,
     and homes, and left many without access to basic
     services. It created a humanitarian crisis affecting
     more than 119,000 families (540,000 persons) that
     were forced to seek shelter in evacuation centers or
     with relatives in neighboring towns. About 77,000
     families (more than 350,000 persons) were displaced
     in Marawi City and neighboring municipalities.
     The Marawi Siege left 128 persons dead and 336
     missing. This incident highlighted the need for a
     comprehensive approach to address the underlying
     causes of extremism and terrorism in the region.

Key Actions                                                                    Responsible Stakeholders

  a    Conduct assessments for scanning and warning terrorism threats,         DRRMCs, NCMC, ATC
       ideally at all levels, for preparedness for response.


  b    Implement anticipatory actions based on heightened risks,               DRRMCs
       indicated by forecasts.


  c    Activate risk- and forecast-based financing to support early and        DRRMCs, DSWD, DILG
       anticipatory actions of LGUs and agencies.


  d    Issue timely, accurate and reliable information, protocols, warnings,   OCD, Advisory Agencies (NCMC, ATC), and DICT
       and public advisories, and ensure functionality of communications


  e    Activate information platforms from humanitarian actors.                OCD, Philippine International Humanitarian
                                                                               Assistance (PIHA)


  f    Coordinate advance prepositioning to ensure readiness of                Response and Early Recovery Pillar
       response facilities, appropriate agencies, regional offices, and
       LGUs for the activation of evacuation plans, systems, and
       procedures.


  g    Organize response teams.                                                Response and Early Recovery Pillar


  h    Establish children-, women-, older persons, and persons with            DSWD, DHSUD
       disability-friendly transitional shelters and referral system for
       orphaned, unaccompanied, and separated children.


  i    Conduct pre-emptive evacuation for communities, livestock,              LGUs, DILG
       pets, and other livelihood assets forecasted to be affected to
       safer grounds based on risk assessment, and other information/
       documents

Procedures                                                               3.3 Impact Phase
1.   The NCMC, ATC, and relevant technical experts and                   The activities in the Impact Phase start with an advisory from
     agencies on terrorism and national security shall provide           the NCMC/ATC about the occurrence of terrorism-related
     a recommendation to the OCD (for national/regional                  incidents causing harm and/or displacement of communities. It is
     operations) or Local DRRM office (for local operations) if          expected that requests from affected regions will commence and
     the situation necessitates convening the DRRMC.                     will be received by the EOC. Though augmentation of resources
                                                                         happens in the Impact Phase, best practices require that even
2.   The National/Regional DRRMC through the OCD shall                   before disaster strikes, clusters should preposition resources.
     convene the members of the Response and Early
     Recovery Pillar. At the local level, it shall be initiated by the   Key actions for this disaster phase, and corresponding internal
     Local DRRMCs through Local DRRM Offices. Any DRRMC                  stakeholders are summarized in the succeeding table, followed
     may initiate the conduct of a pillar meeting to discuss the         by the procedures, and supporting guidelines on how to
     situation of the threat/crisis without the need to wait for a       operationalize these actions.
     higher DRRMC to organize a meeting. The Chairperson
     of the DRRMC shall have the final discretion to call for a
     meeting.

3.   The pillar meetings shall be attended by the members
     of the Response and Early Recovery. Other agencies
     and non-government stakeholders may be invited, as
     necessary, with the approval of the Chairperson. Expenses
     for the conduct of meetings and related activities in the
     context of pre-impact/pre-emergency shall be charged
     against the Quick Response Fund (QRF).

4.   At the local level, the results of assessments for scanning
     and warning terrorism threats must be immediately
     reported by the Chairperson of the DRRMC or CMC to the
     next higher DRRMC level to anticipate potential situation
     escalation.

5.   As directed by the Responsible Official, the Emergency
     Operations Center (EOC) will be activated.
     » Refer to NDRRMC MC No. 131 s.2018 for Guidelines
       on Establishment and Management of EOC
     » Refer to NDRRMC Memorandum Circular No. 05, s.
       2022 for guidelines on the Virtual EOC in case physical
       reporting is unsafe for personnel.
6.   The Chairperson/Executive Director of the DRRMC or the
     Chairperson of the CMC or their representative should
     designate officials in the areas to be affected pursuant to
     the ATC Strategic Communication Plan and NDRRMC
     Communication Plan. The designated official shall be the
     focal contact person should a terrorist strike happen and
     shall provide necessary information to guide activities for
     the Impact Phase.

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 113
Procedures
1.   The Post-Conflict Needs Assessment (PCNA) is to be
     conducted, subject to situation analysis, upon declaration
     of transition to Post-Impact Phase by the Chairperson of
     the NDRRMC, the Executive Director of the NDRRMC,
     and/or the Vice-Chairperson (Response) of the NDRRMC.

2.   The NDRRMC through the OCD will organize PDNA
     Teams that will prepare all documents and available
     data to facilitate a speedy assessment in accordance
     with NDRRMC guidelines. Refer to the following links for
     additional information:
     » OCD-CDTI Training Course Design for capacity-
       building courses and materials for PDNA
     » Ready to Rebuild Manual for supplementary
       information on PDNA and Disaster Rehabilitation and
       Recovery Planning

3.   The period for Early Recovery shall commence provided
     that ALL of the conditions listed below are met.
     • Overall priority of emergency management has shifted
       from life-saving to life sustaining.
     • Emergency situation has been stabilized and
       operations in affected areas are completely safe and
       allow for the conduct of humanitarian assistance.
     • Present number of displaced populations has peaked
       and there is an observed and sustained downward
       trend.
     • Uninterrupted disaster relief system is established
       to sustain provision of food and non-food items to
       affected families.

4.   The NCMC, AFP, ATC, technical agencies such as
     DOST-PHIVOLCS, DOST-PAGASA, DENR-MGB, DOH,
     and other expert agencies and organizations shall
     provide technical advice to determine whether it is safe to
     commence Early Recovery operations.
     » Refer to NDRRMC Memorandum No. 80, s.2022
                                                                   - termination of early recovery period;
       (Disaster Early Recovery Guidelines and Creation of the
       Early Recovery Cluster)                                     - deactivation and demobilization of Response Clusters
                                                                     and IMT;
5.   The NDRRMC through the NDRRMC Chairperson/
     Executive Director/Vice-Chairperson for Response and          - downscaling of alert level; and
     Early Recovery, upon recommendation of the Early              - transition to disaster rehabilitation and recovery.

concerns of all government agencies involved in disaster             International Humanitarian Cluster (PIHAC)
management. Conducting efficient SRR operations during
disasters relies on the timely and organized deployment of SRR       C. Legal Basis
Teams.

will deal directly with the needs and operational requirements             of 2021";
of SRR teams, in coordination with other Response Clusters
and affected Local Government Unit/s (LGU/s). The national           2.    Executive Order (EO) No. 24 series of 2023 entitled,
organization will coordinate and deploy all available SRR teams            "Constituting the Disaster Response and Crisis
from the government, civil society, private sector, and the                Management Task Force";

B. Scope and Limitation                                              D. Cluster Objectives
Specifically designed for national-level responses to disasters      The SRR Cluster aims to provide support for effective, timely,
caused by hydrometeorological events, earthquakes, tsunamis,         organized and systematic search, rescue, and retrieval
and terrorism-related incidents, these guidelines outline the        operations in affected areas for all emergencies in order to
roles and responsibilities of the SRR Cluster Lead and member        minimize further loss of lives and casualties. This includes the
agencies throughout each phase of disaster response (pre-            hand-over of casualties to the Health Cluster for proper treatment
impact, impact, and post-impact).                                    and management, as well as to the Management of Dead and
                                                                     Missing (MDM) for accounting and management of deceased
These guidelines do not include the internal procedures or           individuals and missing persons. The SRR Cluster is the national
actions of each member agency.                                       organization that will coordinate and deploy all available Search
                                                                     and Rescue teams from the government, civil society, private
These guidelines also do not cover the acceptance and                sector, and the international community.
management of foreign SRR teams conducting operations within
the country. Procedures for management of foreign military and
assets shall follow the standards set by the AFP Multinational
Coordinating Centre (MNCC) and the Civil-Military Coordinating
Centre (CMCC).

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 137
C. Process

                                                   SRR RESPONSE CYCLE

 Preparedness            SRR teams conduct training and exercises, review lessons learned from previous experiences, update
                         Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and Contingency Plans as needed, and plan for future disaster
                         responses to ensure readiness for potential deployment.

 Mobilization            SRR teams prepare for immediate response to affected areas by readying personnel and equipment for
                         rapid deployment.

 Operations              SRR teams deploy to perform search, rescue, and retrieval operations in disaster-affected areas, executing
                         missions according to established protocols.

 Demobilization          SRR teams terminate operations in coordination with the Emergency Operations Center (EOC). SRR teams
                         submit all completed documents to the EOC before departure.

 Post-Mission            SRR teams return to their home base, complete and submit a post-mission report within 45 days of
                         operation termination, and conduct a lessons-learned review to enhance future response effectiveness and
                         efficiency. The post-mission report is provided to the secretariat within the specified period.


Activation                                                          Deactivation

The Mobilization and Operations part of the SRR Response            Demobilization and Post-Mission part of the SRR Cycle shall
Cycle shall commence upon the activation of the Response            commence upon the deactivation of the Response Cluster of the
Cluster of the NDRRMC through a memorandum. The SRR                 NDRRMC through a memorandum.
Cluster shall be convened as deemed necessary.
                                                                    D. Concept of Sustainment
Reporting
                                                                    The AFP as SRR Cluster lead will coordinate all support,
All member agencies of the SRR cluster are required to submit       requirements, and requests for augmentation at the affected
their response reports and updates to the AFP, which serves as      areas during the disaster. Concerned government agencies
the SRR cluster lead. The AFP will then consolidate and package     and their subordinate offices shall utilize their respective
these reports into a SRR cluster report for the information of      internal personnel. Additional personnel requirements shall
the Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Response and Chairperson,         be coordinated through the NDRRMC in collaboration with all
NDRRMC.                                                             Council members.

Reporting channels and timelines indicated in the NDRRMC
Response Clusters activation and deactivation memorandum
shall be observed in the submission of reports.

A. Rationale                                                           memo states that the DICT "shall ensure the attainment of the
                                                                       Cluster’s objective to strengthen ICT capacities at the national
Located in the Pacific Ring of Fire and the Typhoon Belt, the          Cluster level down to local levels to prepare for, respond to and
Philippines is highly vulnerable to natural disasters such as          recover from the impacts of disaster".
typhoons, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, floods, landslides,
and the like. Communications play a vital role in all response         The purpose of these guidelines is to provide an
and rehabilitation efforts in disaster operations. However, there      overarching framework for effective and timely emergency

operational.                                                           majority of ETC key players are based in the field, and call on

The government seeks to strengthen communications by
establishing an interoperable and seamless telecommunications          B. Scope and Limitation
platform. This will contribute to effective and efficient
management of emergency response; maintenance of                       This document seeks to provide organized and efficient services,
command, control, and situational awareness; and unification of        with emphasis on the responsibilities of the Department on
operations.                                                            the coordination and collaboration at the local DRRMOs to
                                                                       organize, train, and equip the local government emergency
In line with this, the activation of the Emergency                     telecommunications team (GETT) to align with the National
Telecommunications Cluster (ETC) is crucial to maintain a system       and Regional GETT and strengthen the exercising leadership
of communications and to respond to emergency situations.              by providing command and control functions from the national

(NDRP) developed by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and           C. Legal Bases

D. Cluster Objectives                                                II. CONCEPT OF OPERATIONS
The Emergency Telecommunications Cluster (ETC) aims to
provide effective and timely emergency telecommunications            A. Command and Control
services and ICT capacities at the national level down to local
levels in areas affected by disaster.                                1.   Command Relationship – all DRRM actors and key players
                                                                          will refer and base their actions as prescribed in IRR of R.A.
This will be organized by providing timely, resilient, and                10121, Rule 3, Section 2. Composition
predictable Information and Communications Technology (ICT)
support to improve:                                                  2.   Command Center – National Disaster Risk Reduction and
                                                                          Management Operations Center (NDRRMOC), Camp
•    Operability: Establish communications in safe and reliable           General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City
     conditions and ensure sustainability of communications in
     support of mission-essential operations according to pre-            a. Succession of Command:
     defined operational requirements.
                                                                           » (1) Emergency Telecommunications Cluster Lead (DICT-
•    Interoperability: Ensure capability to exchange and
                                                                             DRRMD)
     make use of information through communication among
     various levels of government, through the use of diversified          » (2) Emergency Telecommunications Cluster Co-Lead
     technologies such as radio frequency, satellite technologies,           (OCD-ICTD)
     charging stations, and other emerging technologies and                » (3) Emergency Telecommunications Cluster Members
     services as needed and as authorized.
                                                                          b. Interagency Communication System Support
•    Sustenance of Communication Flows: Maintain
                                                                     3.   For the duration of the operations, the existing means of
     communications in the event of disaster or devastation of the
                                                                          communications or whatever is applicable shall be utilized.
     primary communications infostructure.
                                                                          However, the ETC will be activated once communication
•    ICT Emergency Assistance: Provide other ICT                          from affected areas is cut.
     support to the affected community and augment much
     needed telecommunications services specifically when            B. Roles and Functions
     communication systems are down.

 AGENCY                      DISASTER        Hydrometeorological Hazards; Earthquakes and Tsunamis; Consequence
                             PHASE           Management of Terrorism-Related Incidents

 LEAD:                       Pre-Impact      a. Act as the single focal point of national and/or local government authorities
                                                on behalf of the humanitarian community for all radio, voice, and data
 DEPARTMENT OF                                  communications-related capabilities including frequency allocation,
 INFORMATION                                    communications network diagrams, and mobilization of ICT resources.
 COMMUNICATIONS
                                             b. Alert and organize the Government Emergency Telecommunications Teams
 TECHNOLOGY (DICT)
                                                (GETT) and conduct immediate inventory of their ICT equipment on-hand.
 through the Disaster
 Risk Reduction and                          c. Pre-program all radios to the assigned disaster frequencies and ensure
 Management Division                            communications interoperability.
 (DRRMD)
                                             d. Provide radio nets and call signs and manage the frequencies allocated by
                                                NTC.
                                             e. Provide a platform directory of services containing the contact information of
                                                key ICT players.
                                             f.   In coordination with Logistics Cluster, make available mobility assets, protective
                                                  suits/gears, shelters/Tents, basic provisions, and other essential needs of the
                                                  GETT for the establishment of on-site EOCs.
                                             g. Provide standards for common ICT equipment and procedures.
                                             h. Provide ICT services support for other clusters as needed.
                                             i.   Provide support by sending warning advisories to OCDRC/RDRRMC and
                                                  LGUs, NDRRMC member agencies.
                                             j.   Collect information regarding the impending disaster and corresponding
                                                  situations of preparedness by LGUs and related agencies and inform/report
                                                  them to N/RDRRMCs.
                                             k. Conduct scenario-based pre-deployment planning meeting in a predefined
                                                location/s.
                                             l.   Collect and record all activities of GETT members for review and
                                                  documentation.

                             Impact          a. Provide basic telecommunications coverage for the Command Center and
                                                other responders around the main operational area.
                                             b. Establish and maintain On-Site Command Operations Coordinating Center
                                                (OSOCC) in disaster area/s through the deployment of GETT.
                                             c. Program radios or other equipment belonging to individual responders,
                                                organizations, and other key players in disaster areas to ensure interoperability.
                                             d. Install and maintain internal ICT systems, independent of national or local public
                                                services, and between clusters and individuals.
                                             e. Establish a dedicated GSM/LTE mobile network to be used by cluster workers
                                                in the operational area in partnership with TELCO(s).
                                             f.   Publish and maintain a list of GSM providers, availability of 2G/3G/4G/LTE and
                                                  data services, and availability and reliability in partnership with TELCO(s).
                                             g. Provide and update a service directory with the contact information of key
                                                players in disaster areas of a specific humanitarian mission.

C. Process                                                               D. Concept of Sustainment
Following orders, the ETC will provide effective and timely                  1.   The DICT as Cluster Lead and OCD as Co-Lead will
emergency telecommunications services and ICT capacities                          coordinate the dispatch of the GETT and will handle all its
at the national Cluster level down to local levels in the affected                needs to support its operation in the affected area.
disaster areas.
                                                                             2.   All members of the cluster acting on request from the
This will be accomplished through the deployment of the GETT                      DICT will make available necessary and operational
which will provide timely, resilient, and predictable ICT support.                telecommunication equipment at the disposal of the GETT
DICT will act as lead agency and OCD as co-lead agency                            in the affected area.
supported by AFP, PNP, BFP, PCG, NTC, DSWD, and other
NGOs, CSOs, Volunteer Groups, and other Civilian and Private                 3.   Concerned government agencies and their subordinate
Organizations recognized and acknowledge by NDRRMC. GETT                          offices shall utilize their respective personnel. Additional
will support other clusters in safely and efficiently carrying out                resources and funding requirements shall be coordinated
their respective mandates as they prepare for, respond to, and                    through the NDRRMC in collaboration with all Council
help in recovery from the impacts of disasters.                                   members.

At the end of the operations, when full normalcy of the
telecommunication system services of the affected areas is
attained, GETT will be deactivated and will follow formal hand-
over of the ICT responsibility to concerned government units.

response was given importance to be in sync current trends

of the NDRPs, and focuses on specific disaster response
logistics operations before, during, and after disaster. The               e. It will cover the different roles of the agencies in the

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 169
B. Roles and Functions


AGENCY   DISASTER     Hydrometeorological Hazards         Earthquakes and Tsunamis             Consequence Management of
         PHASE                                                                                 Terrorism-Related Incidents


OCD      Pre-Impact   a. Prepares database of all Logistics Cluster assets, equipment, resources, and focal persons (List of
                         personnel, trainings, inventory of equipment, current activities/plans)
                      b. Provides standards for common logistics management equipment and procedures that includes
                         agreements, Memorandum of Understanding (MOU), Memorandum of Agreements (MOAs) as part
                         of the short to long term actions
                      c. Conduct series of meetings with the members of the Logistics Cluster for the improvement of
                         coordination and strengthening of collaboration
                      d. Create policies and guidelines relative to the actions undertaken by the Logistics Cluster, ensuring
                         seamlessness of disaster response and interoperability

         Impact       a. Coordinate transportation requests to available service providers
                      b. Facilitate all other logistics-related requests, i.e. RDANA helicopter assets, deployment of water
                         filtration teams, and other similar activities
                      c. Provide coordination support and process requests that will be received by the NDRRMOC
                      d. Provide petroleum, oil, and fuel for response vehicles
                      e. Coordinate with RDRRMCs regarding status of power lines, water connection, and power lines in
                         affected areas
                      f.   Consolidate all activities conducted by the Logistics Cluster member agencies and submits a
                           consolidated report to the NDRRMC Cluster Coordinator

         Post-        a. Conduct reverse logistics and gradual demobilization of assets
         Impact
                      b. Coordinates the preparation of documentation for the Logistics Cluster requirements, lesson
                         learned, and best practices;
                      c. Continues implementation of capability development programs (organizational enhancement,
                         training, procurement of equipment)

DSWD     Pre-Impact   a. Conduct of regular inventory monitoring
                      b. Creation of Memorandum of Agreements or Understanding to improve logistics capabilities
                      c. Prepositioning and stockpiling of relief items in accordance to DSWD’s role as lead, Food and Non-
                         Food Items Cluster

         Impact       a. Assists in inventory management through the Food and Non-food Items Cluster
                      b. Perform repacking of Food Packs
                      c. Identify immediate logistics needs in evacuation centers and camps through the Camp Coordination
                         and Camp Management Cluster
                      d. Provides the availability of DSWD warehouses in the Regions and LGUs for storage of food and non-
                         food items
                      e. Assists in the transportation of DSWD relief items using their transportation vehicles

             PHASE                                                                                    Terrorism-Related Incidents

 DSWD        Post-        a. Perform repair and restoration of storage facilities (if damaged)
             Impact
                          b. Conduct monitoring and evaluation of the activities performed by DSWD
                          c. Replenishment of stocks in accordance with organization’s policies and procedures
                          d. Conduct reverse logistics and gradual demobilization of assets

 AFP-DND     Pre-Impact   a. Requirements determination of assets in the areas likely to be affected by disasters.
                          b. Planning, prepositioning of assets needed for transportation of supplies and equipment
                          c. Direct coordination to other agencies and stakeholders to support the transportation of supplies
                          d. Identify priority areas for the distribution of relief and coordinate with local government units for the
                             requirements of the people.
                          e. Determine the vehicle requirements for the hauling of relief goods and other supplies

             Impact       a. Identify the storage facilities for the warehousing of relief goods
                          b. Safeguard the resources for distribution
                          c. Hauling of resources and other relief goods to the affected area through the use of dedicated AFP
                             assets
                          d. Augment emergency transportation by providing military vehicles, aircraft, and vessels that have
                             flexible mobility and capacity of mass transportation
                          e. Assist in the distribution of relief goods
                          f.   Management of the distribution of relief goods
                          g. Perform lateral coordination with other stakeholders.

             Post-        a. Perform post-operations review and gather the lessons learned and best practices of the AFP.
             Impact
                          b. Gap identification and assessment
                          c. Conduct of debriefing sessions.

 DOTr        Impact       To lead the Transportation Cluster in the mobilization and deployment of transportation needs in times of
                          emergencies
             Post-
             Impact

                          services to the affected areas.
             Post-
             Impact

 LTO         Pre-Impact   To perform the inspection of all motor vehicles committed by other member agencies to ensure that
                          these vehicles are roadworthy and safe to be utilized by people. To escort the delivery of relief goods,
                          personnel, and equipment.

 AGENCY      DISASTER     Hydrometeorological Hazards           Earthquakes and Tsunamis            Consequence Management of
             PHASE                                                                                  Terrorism-Related Incidents

 NFA         Pre-Impact   During pre-impact NFA buys palay from individual farmers/farmer organizations at the government
                          support price. This program is undertaken to ensure and establish manageable buffer stock in line with
                          the agency function of maintaining optimal level of rice inventory that shall be maintained at any given
                          time to be used for emergency situations and to sustain the disaster relief programs of the government
                          during national or man-made calamities.

             Impact       a. NFA Distribution – It is the program of strategic positioning of the stocks in order to respond to relief
                             operations of the government and also for the replacement of stocks.
                          b. For emergency use: Rice releases in response to emergencies in support of disaster relief programs
                             of the government through sales: Government Agencies, LGU and Private Institutions involved in
                             relief operations.

             Post         As stated under R.A 11203 Section 8 Maintenance of Rice Buffer Stocks the NFA shall, in accordance
             Impact       with the rules, regulations and procedures to be promulgated, maintain sufficient rice buffer stock to be
                          sourced safety from local farmers.

 CAAP        Pre-Impact   a. Conduct Capacity Development activities on getting airports ready to manage increased influx of
                             passenger and cargo
                          b. Identify airports that are capable to receive relief flights
                          c. Establish or keep current – directory of determined stakeholders during disasters

             Impact       a. Help coordinate aviation agreements and other security needs for the transportation of relief goods,
                             personnel, and equipment (through CAB-OTS)
                          b. Activate learned procedures and determined teams to perform procedures
                          c. Activate coordination with stakeholders
                          d. To provide assistance with regard to air travel operations as well as the inspection of aircraft being
                             utilized for mobilization of goods or equipment

             Post         a. Implement learned procedures
             Impact
                          b. Continue active and responsive coordination with all stakeholders
                          c. Conduct Post Activity Meeting for Performance Review and update of procedures

 PCG         Pre-Impact   a. Participate in the development of national local disasters plans
                          b. Participate in Risk Assessment and Planning
                          c. Enforce safety regulations to prevent accidents and casualties

             Impact       a. To augment personnel for safe maritime transport and provide assets such as vessels and

                          b. Enforce safety regulations to prevent accidents and casualties

ANNEX A.
INVENTORY OF RESOURCES

                                                                                     188 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
Debris Clearing and Civil Works Cluster
OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES




I. INTRODUCTION                                                        •   This document covers the clearing/removal and handling of
                                                                           disaster debris and civil works (e.g., provision of temporary
                                                                           detour roads, bridges, and slope protection). However, it
                                                                           does not include debris on private property, reuse/recycling,
A. Rationale
                                                                           and segregation activities.
The Philippines is a high-risk country and disaster-prone due to       •   This document is primarily intended for use by the Debris
its geographic location. Disaster events from hydrometeorological          Clearing and Civil Works Cluster (DCCW). Therefore, it
and geological hazards generate huge amounts of debris                     should be cascaded down to the Regional, Provincial, City,
causing blockage of road systems, disruption of services, and              and Municipal Emergency Operations Centers (EOCs) of
other problems. Enormous amounts of debris such as soil                    the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Councils in
erosions, wrecked vehicles, carcasses, and damaged lifeline                support of the Cluster/Sectors in disaster-affected areas.
structures are inevitable during these events, thus, debris clearing
and civil works clusters are established.                              •   This document will not cover the procedures for providing
                                                                           debris-clearing assistance to the Philippines, which may
Proper management of disaster debris diminishes the associated             extend to other countries affected by disasters.
risks posed by a disaster to ensure the public’s safety. It will
provide ease of access for emergency response operations and           •   This document is generally applicable to different types of
emergency/critical facilities and will contribute to unhampered            disasters or emergencies. The procedures presented herein
mobility of the affected area. The debris clearing may vary among          are applicable during preparedness and response and early
local government units and government agencies. However, the               recovery operations for hydrometeorological, geological, and
common denominator is the need for organized and collaborative             human-induced hazards.
efforts.
                                                                       •   Seeing the complexity of response for human-induced
                                                                           incidents such as terrorism-related incidents and chemical,
B. Scope and Limitation
                                                                           biological, radiologic, and nuclear (CBRN) incidents, debris-
                                                                           clearing activities will need to be calibrated based on the
The scope and limitations of these Operational Guidelines are
                                                                           advice of the NDRRMC Chairperson, Executive Director, and
listed below.
                                                                           Vice-Chairperson for Response, prioritizing the safety and
•   This Operational Guideline addresses the use of the                    security of all Cluster personnel, assets, and resources.
    resources of the cluster member agencies in times of
                                                                       •   The Cluster’s activities may be extended in times of
    disasters and other national emergencies.
                                                                           protracted relief operations up to rehabilitation operations
•   This document will not include agency-level procedures                 unless otherwise directed for termination.
    and protocols currently implemented in the respective
                                                                       •   These Operational Guidelines shall be subject to continuous
    Cluster Member Agencies. The guideline, for all intents and
                                                                           development and enhancement based on documented
    purposes, is not intended to promote abrupt changes to
                                                                           lessons learned and practice.
    organizational processes. However, it can be used as a
    reference for calibrating agency processes concerning inter-
    agency collaboration related to emergency debris clearing.




2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 189
C. Legal Bases                                                        E. Composition
•   Republic Act (RA) No.10121, Philippine Disaster Risk
    Reduction and Management (DRRM) Act of 2010;                       Lead       Department of Public Works and Highways
                                                                                  (DPWH)
•   National Disaster Response Plans (NDRPs);
                                                                       Member     •   Department of Transportation (DOTr)
•   National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan
                                                                       Agencies
    (NDRRMP) 2020 – 2030;                                                         •   Philippine Coast Guard (PCG)
                                                                                  •   Philippine Ports Authority (PPA)
D. Cluster Objectives
                                                                                  •   Civil Aviation Authority of the Philippines
The Debris Clearing and Civil Works Cluster is mandated to                            (CAAP)
remove all disaster debris that obstructs major thoroughfares,                    •   Department of the Interior and Local
emergency operations, or access to emergency/critical                                 Government (DILG)
facilities. This cluster shall ensure unhampered mobility of
goods and services and assist responders in penetrating the                       •   Department of Information and
disaster-affected areas by providing engineering support in the                       Communications Technology (DICT)
assessment of infrastructure and facilities.                                      •   Department of Energy (DOE)

The objectives of this cluster are the following:                                 •   National Electrification Administration (NEA)
                                                                                  •   National Power Corporation (NPC)
•   Clearing and removal of disaster debris and obstructions
                                                                                  •   National Transmission Corporation (TransCo)
•   Provide temporary access/detour roads and bridges
                                                                                  •   Local Water Utilities Administration (LWUA)
•   Ensure unhampered mobility                                                    •   Department of Environment and Natural
•   Provide temporary service facilities and restoration of service                   Resources (DENR)
    facilities (e.g., ports and utilities)                                        •   Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP)

•   Readiness and coordination among DCCW Cluster member                          •   Philippine National Police (PNP)
    agencies and offices                                                          •   Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP)
•   Provide accurate information to Response Cluster members                      •   Metro Manila Development Authority of the
                                                                                      Philippines (MMDA)
                                                                                  •   Utility Service Providers (Electricity, Water,
                                                                                      and Telecommunications)
                                                                                  •   Philippines Red Cross (PRC)
                                                                                  •   Non-Government Organization (NSO)
                                                                                  •   Civil Society Organization (CSO)
                                                                                  •   Volunteer Groups




                                                                                       190 | 2024 National Disaster Response Plan
The Cluster shall include the participation of other government
and non-government agencies/offices/organizations deemed
necessary or relevant in achieving the Cluster's objectives, roles,
responsibilities, and deliverables.




II. CONCEPT OF OPERATIONS

A. Command and Control
1.    Command Relationship

      a. All DRRM actors and key players will refer to and base
         their actions as prescribed in the Implementing Rules
         and Regulations (IRR) of RA 10121, Rule 3, Section 2.
         Composition

2.    Command Center

      a. National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
         Operations Center (NDRRMOC) Building, Office of Civil
         Defense, Camp General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City.

3.    Succession of Command

      a. DCCW Cluster Lead (DPWH)
      b. DCCW Cluster Alternate Lead (DOTr or DILG)
      c. DCCW Cluster Members




2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 191
B. Roles and Function


AGENCY       DISASTER       Hydrometeorological                Earthquakes and Tsunamis          Consequence Management
             PHASE          Hazards                                                              of Terrorism-Related
                                                                                                 Incidents


DPWH         Pre-Impact     a. Ensure routine maintenance activities are implemented on national roads and bridges (e.g.,
             Phase             pruning of trees and declogging of canals and waterways)
                            b. Prepositioning of available Quick Response Assets (QRA) and manpower to national roads
                               and bridges (for slow onset hazards only)
                            c. Prepare road warning signs / informative signs, and barriers
                            d. Ensure availability of materials for emergency response activities (e.g., sandbags, blue sheets,
                               and modular bridge)
                            e. Lead in the development, implementation, improvement of guidelines, procedures, policies,
                               and plans of the Cluster.
                            f.   Conduct cluster meetings regularly
                            g. Inform the cluster members on the activation of the DCCW Cluster

             Impact Phase   a. Monitor the status of national roads and bridges
                            b. Install road warning signs/informative signs on affected national roads and bridges
                            c. Conduct the removal of obstructions and debris on national roads, bridges and/or rights-of-
                               ways
                            d. Deployment of equipment and manpower on debris collection and removal
                            e. Identify alternate routes for affected national roads/bridges
                            f.   Provide temporary structures to ensure continuous mobility of response operations (e.g.,
                                 detour roads, modular bridge, and slope protection for affected national roads and bridges
                            g. Provide traffic management on national roads and bridges
                            h. Coordinate and consolidate all reports and activities of the cluster members for submission to
                               the Response Cluster head

             Post-Impact    a. Continuous removal of obstructions and debris on national roads, bridges and/or rights-of-
             Phase             ways
                            b. Constant deployment of equipment on debris collection and removal
                            c. Continuous provision of temporary structures to ensure constant mobility of response
                               operations (e.g., detour roads, modular bridges, and slope protection for affected national
                               roads and bridges)
                            d. Continuous traffic management on national roads and bridges
                            e. Conduct of damage assessment on affected national infrastructures
                            f.   Assist with the debris removal activities on local roads and rights-of-ways in coordination with
                                 DILG and LGU
                            g. Coordinate and consolidate all reports and activities of the cluster members for submission to
                               the Response Cluster head

                                 a. Participate in meetings and activities of the DCCW cluster

         Impact Phase   a. Assist in coordinating with the concerned LGUs as necessary.
                        b. Assist in coordinating with the concerned LGUs for the conduct of demolition of obstruction,
                           debris removal activities on local roads and rights-of-ways
                        c. Coordinate with the concerned affected LGUs to mobilize local utility companies to clear
                           debris and restore services in their jurisdictions.
                        d. Information dissemination on alternate routes due to damaged provincial/local roads and
                           bridges, and road obstructions (landslides, toppled power lines, debris and wastes, fallen
                           vegetation, among others.)

         Phase
                        b. Participate in DCCW cluster meetings.

DICT     Pre-Impact     a. Preposition telecommunication assets in the nearest DICT Regional or Provincial Office.
         Phase
                        b. Coordinate with the Private Telecommunication Entities (PTEs) and other Government
                           Agencies on the possible areas of concern that will be affected by the natural hazard(s).
                        c. Participate in meetings and activities of the DCCW cluster.

         Impact Phase   a. Secure and provide communications for the Cluster.
                        b. Coordinate with the telecommunication providers on the rapid damage assessment being
                           conducted by the RDANA Team.
                        c. Coordinate with utility companies/ concessionaires on debris clearing under their jurisdiction.
                        d. Ensure immediate restoration of telecommunication services.

         Post-Impact    a. Submit a final report to DPWH as cluster lead.
         Phase
                        b. Participate in DCCW cluster meetings.

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 197
B. Process                                                                       in support of the debris-clearing operations, ensuring
                                                                                 the safety, welfare, integrity, and condition of the
General Guidelines                                                               resources, assets, property, and personnel accounted
                                                                                 to the Cluster.
     1. Upon order by the NDRRMC and the Vice-Chairperson               Triggers for Activation and Deactivation
        for the Response Cluster, the DCCW Cluster will lead in
        the activation, operations, management, and monitoring                1. Activation
        of all activities. The same principle shall also apply to the
        Regional DRRMCs.                                                         a. DCCW Cluster shall be activated upon the issuance
                                                                                    of an NDRRMC Memorandum signed by the
     2. Debris removal priorities must start from main                              Secretary of the DSWD in their capacity as the Vice-
        thoroughfares, especially roads leading to emergency                        Chairperson for Disaster Response.
        services and facilities, hospitals and other medical

         Impact       a. Activate and deploy Quick Response Teams.
                      b. Activate and deploy pool of volunteers for repacking, delivery of goods, management of donations,
                         and monitoring and distribution of FNI.
                      c. Activate 24/7 operation of Disaster Response Operations and Monitoring and Information Center
                         (DROMIC) at central office and field offices.
                      d. Continue disaster response monitoring and coordination to mobilize instrumentalities and entities
                         of the LGUs, civil society organizations (CSOs), and private groups and organize volunteers for
                         response.
                      e. Provide emergency relief (FNI and drinking water) inside and outside evacuation center.
                      f.   Determine logistical requirements for FNI (warehouse and transport), coordinate with Logistics
                           Cluster and other humanitarian and commercial partner.
                      g. Render duty at the Philippine International Humanitarian Assistance Reception Center (PIHARC)
                         once activated.
                      h. Coordinate with internal donor/donating agencies and assist in the allocation of goods to affected
                         areas.
                      i.   Allocate/augment additional Quick Response Fund (QRF) to the Field Office for purchase of welfare
                           goods, and for operational and administrative costs.
                      j.   Participate in the conduct of Rapid Damage Assessment and Needs Analysis (RDANA), specifically
                           the immediate needs for food and non-food items requirement.
                      k. Coordinate with the Law and Order (LAO) Cluster to ensure the security of relief goods and safety of
                         volunteers and responders.
                      l.   Implement Contingency Plan when needed.

         Post-        a. Participate in the conduct of Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (PDNA) with partner agencies.
         Impact
                      b. Develop a post-distribution monitoring system. Evaluate quality, sufficiency, effectiveness, and
                         timeliness of distributions to help improve the overall distribution system and approach.
                      c. Establish complaints mechanisms to ensure affected families can voice their complaints and the
                         proper agency can take action. In case of fraud, theft, or abuse, camp residents/families must
                         be able to voice their complaints and must know that the lead and coordinating agency including
                         service providers will take action.
                      d. Provide "Pabaon Package" (provision of food packs and other FNI kits) to those who want to go
                         back to their respective places of origin.
                      e. Provide continuing relief assistance when needed.

2024 National Disaster Response Plan | 215
C. Process                                                           D. Concept of Sustainment
1.   The FNI Cluster shall convene to account for all resources      1.   The DSWD as Cluster Lead, using the support of the
     from cluster members, including all FNI that can be tapped           Logistics Cluster, will coordinate all movement of FNI
     whenever necessary.                                                  including those coming from other public or private
                                                                          organizations.
2.   The FNI Cluster shall augment the resources of the
     affected LGUs (City/Municipal/Province) during the pre-         2.   Concerned government agencies and their subordinate
     impact, impact, and post-impact phases. LGUs must have               offices shall utilize their respective internal personnel.
     prepositioned resources that will first be distributed to the        Additional personnel requirements shall be coordinated
     affected population to ensure that the IDPs are provided             through the NDRRMC in collaboration with all Council
     with needed FNI.                                                     members.

3.   The Cluster Lead shall coordinate, allocate, orchestrate,
     and monitor the delivery of FNI to affected families and
     communities.

4.   All members of the FNI Cluster shall coordinate and submit
     reports on their relief distribution/augmentation to the
     Cluster Lead during activation of the response cluster.

5.   The FNI Cluster will follow the cluster approach taking into
     account that the direction of operations will be guided by
     the information provided at the local DRRMCs.

6.   The local DRRMCs should establish an information
     management system for the inventory of available
     resources and distribution of relief goods to IDPs. The
     system will be used to submit requests of resources to
     the next higher level of government for augmentation of
     requirements.

7.   The Cluster Lead shall ensure the safety and well-being
     of the responders before and after going to the disaster
     operations in an affected area.

8.   The DSWD as Cluster Lead, using the support of the
     Logistics Center, shall coordinate all movement of FNI
     including those coming from other public or private
     organizations.

OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES



I. INTRODUCTION                                                                 3.    RA 10821 Children Emergency and Relief and Protection
                                                                                      Act

                                                                                4.    Operation LISTO Disaster Preparedness Manual 2018 for
A. Rationale                                                                          City and Municipal LGUs

The Camp Coordination and Camp Management (CCCM)
                                                                                5.    DSWD MC 006, s.2015 Guidelines in the Institutionalization
cluster aims to provide timely and well-coordinated humanitarian
                                                                                      of Women-Friendly Space (WFS) in Camp Coordination
assistance and augmentation support in managing evacuation
                                                                                      and Camp Management
centers and camps and ensure the availability of safe, secure,
and accessible evacuation centers for emergencies and
disasters.                                                                     D. Cluster Objectives

In responding to the consequences brought about by terrorism-                  The CCCM Cluster aims to provide timely and well-coordinated
related incidents or human-induced disasters, CCCM’s                           humanitarian assistance and support in managing evacuation
paramount task is to save lives. It is also critical for responders to         centers to ensure the needs of disaster-affected families are met.
prioritize their safety through decontamination after undergoing
response operations in an area affected by hazardous Chemical,                 Specifically, it aims to:
Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear components.
                                                                                1.    Ensure the availability of identified safe, secure, and
B. Scope and Limitation                                                               accessible evacuation centers for emergencies and
                                                                                      disasters;
These Operational Guidelines shall cover all processes, roles,
and responsibilities of the Camp Coordination and Camp                          2.    Ensure establishment of sex and age-disaggregated data
Management Cluster members at the national level, and other                           e.g., listing and profiling of affected families and IDPs in
relevant government and non-government agencies involved in                           evacuation centers or temporary displacement sites;
providing augmentation support in camp management of LGUs
and in supporting the needs of the affected communities.                        3.    Ensure that all IDPs in evacuation centers are provided
                                                                                      with basic humanitarian needs compliant with the Sphere
C. Legal Bases                                                                        Standards1 such as, but not limited to, food with enough
                                                                                      nutritional value, potable water, clothing, family items,
1.    Joint Memorandum Circular 02 Guideline on Camp                                  hygiene kits, and other essential non-food items;
      Coordination and Camp Management and Internally
      Displaced Persons Protection                                              4.    Ensure provision of MHPSS service to the IDPs inside
                                                                                      evacuation centers; and
2.    DILG MC No. 122 s/ 20218 Guidelines for Local
      Government Units on the Strengthening of Evacuation                       5.    Continuously seek opportunities for early recovery,
      Systems using the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and                             rehabilitation, and developmental tasks as post-response
      Management Fund                                                                 activities are undertaken, in case of prolonged stay.




1    Sphere is a global organization comprised of humanitarian experts that is responsible for establishing humanitarian standards. The Sphere
     Handbook, its principal publication, has gained significant recognition due to its compilation of universal standards and common principles in
     humanitarian response (Sphere)

                                                                       communication shall be utilized where applicable. However, the

 Lead         Department of Social Welfare and                         communication from affected areas is cut.

II. CONCEPT OF OPERATIONS

A. Command and Control
1.   Command Relationship - All DRRM actors and key players
     will refer to and base their actions on prescriptions in IRR of
     R.A. 10121, Rule 3, Section 2. Composition.

2.   Command Center - National Disaster Risk Reduction and
     Management Operations Center (NDRRMOC), Camp
     General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City.

3.   Succession of Command:
     • Chairperson, NDRRMC (Secretary, DND)

     • Vice-Chairperson for Disaster Response (Secretary,
       DSWD)

     • CCCM Cluster Lead (DRMB)

     • CCCM Cluster Members

4.   Inter-agency Communication System Support

C. Process
The CCCM Cluster aims to provide timely and well-coordinated            sustained operations. Such centers include those under the
humanitarian assistance and augmentation support in managing            supervision of the LGU, DepEd, and privately-owned places
the evacuation center so as to ensure that the needs of disaster-       utilized during disasters.
affected families are attended to appropriately.
                                                                        Concerned government agencies and their subordinate offices
The DSWD leads the operation and coordination with other                shall utilize their respective internal personnel to support
partners/stakeholders especially with key players/service               CCCM operation. Additional personnel requirements shall
providers such as DepEd and the DOH, to ensure that facilities          be coordinated through the NDRRMC Response Pillar in
inside the evacuation center are established and basic services         collaboration with all other Response Clusters.
are accessible and available when needed.
                                                                        For natural hazards such as hydrometeorological hazards and
The DSWD as Cluster Lead shall also coordinate all support              earthquakes and tsunamis, the following concept of operations
and requirements of the affected LGUs for evacuation centers'           applies:

                    from the Vice-Chair for Disaster         Orders or directives from the Vice-   Chair for Disaster Response
                    Response                                 Chair for Disaster Response

                                                             Needs Analysis (RDANA) Result
                    Activation Orders or Directives                                                Report on the number of displaced
                    from the Vice-Chair for Disaster         Report on the number of displaced     population inside evacuation center
                    Response                                 population inside evacuation center   Report on number of casualties
                                                                                                   and injuries




D. Concept of Sustainment
The CCCM cluster lead agency shall coordinate and facilitate all        Concerned government agencies and their subordinate offices
requests on camp management and updating of CCCM reports.               shall utilize their respective internal personnel. Additional
                                                                        personnel requirements shall be coordinated through the
All cluster members will provide support, facilitate all needs on       NDRRMC in collaboration with all Council members.
Camp Management, and update reports on CCCM.

OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES



I. INTRODUCTION                                                      3.    Comprehensive Emergency Program for Children (CEPC) is
                                                                           the basis for handling disasters and other emergencies to
                                                                           protect children and pregnant and lactating mothers, and
                                                                           to support them immediately once a local or national state
A. Rationale
                                                                           of calamity is declared
As the government cluster lead for the protection of internally
displaced persons (IDP), the Department of Social Welfare and        D.Cluster Objectives
Development (DSWD) shall put in place effective mechanisms to
prevent sexual violence and provide accessible prompt services       The IDP Protection cluster aims to ensure the provision of
during disasters. In the Philippines, the Protection Cluster has     support, readily accessible assistance, and protection services
two arms: the Child Protection Working Group (CPWG) and the          to the affected internally displaced population and to establish
Gender-Based Violence (GBV) Sub-cluster. The CPWG aims to            a strong mechanism to prevent sexual violence and child
bring together child protection actors and partners operational in   protection issues during disaster operations.
areas affected by both natural and human-induced disasters and
to facilitate the development and coordination of child protection   Specifically, it aims to:
strategies and responses, including advocacy with authorities
and humanitarian actors as necessary. The GBV Sub-cluster            1.    Ensure timely, appropriate, and quality provision of
aims to coordinate and support relevant stakeholders to put in             multi-sectoral and survivor-centered child protection and
place mechanisms to prevent GBV and provide survivor-centered              gender-based violence services in accordance with local,
and appropriate services to GBV survivors during natural and               national, and international guidelines or standards on child
human-induced disasters.                                                   protection and GBV;

B. Scope and Limitation                                              2.    Establish and maintain appropriate coordination
                                                                           mechanisms among all humanitarian actors including
These Operational Guidelines shall cover all processes of the IDP          coordination of protection inputs, reports, humanitarian
Protection Cluster members and other relevant government and               plans, and generation of resources;
non-government agencies involved in providing affected LGUs
augmentation support on protection services and in supporting        3.    Ensure that their protection response adequately takes
the affected communities by ensuring their needs are met.                  into account the primary responsibility of the government
                                                                           to ensure the protection of affected persons by, among
C. Legal Bases                                                             others, establishing adequate response mechanisms and
                                                                           coordination with the national and local authorities;

                 Phase           supplies, equipment and tools, and funds from chapters in preparation for resource
                                 prepositioning.

                                 status in the field through the rapid damage assessment and needs analysis (RDANA).

                 Impact       a. Assist in providing relief supplies such as food (relief goods, hot meals, etc.) and non-food items
                                 (hygiene kits, dignity kits, emergency shelter kits, etc.) to augment relief operation activities in
                                 coordination with the lead cluster and all its members.
                              b. Mobilize the logistical support required to augment response capacity and/or field base
                                 operations managed by the PRC through its chapters.
                              c. Assist in information gathering of the affected population through its chapter welfare desks and
                                 conduct tracing by restoring family links in evacuation centers in coordination with LGU.
                              d. Support the DRRMC Operation Center in gathering and collating disaster reports and statistics.
                              e. Ensure coordination of PRC disaster operations and activities at national headquarters, chapter,
                                 and other field operation levels to the cluster.

                 Post         a. Provide updates and/or situational reports to the NDRRMC through the Food and Non-Food
                 Impact          Cluster (FNF).
                              b. Continuously monitor and coordinate with cluster members as to needs and gaps.

not arise in emergencies and to respond to and mitigate the             such as children. The subsequent figures illustrate the reporting
effect of any protection issues that may arise inside and outside       and referral procedures for GBV-related incidents and instances

D. Concept of Sustainment
The IDP Protection cluster lead agency shall coordinate and
facilitate all requests on camp management and updating of IDP
Protection reports.

All cluster members will provide support, facilitate all needs on
protection service, and update reports on IDP Protection

Concerned government agencies and their subordinate offices
shall utilize their respective internal personnel. Additional
personnel requirements shall be coordinated through the
NDRRMC in collaboration with all Council members.

      affected area.                                               The condition/s that will trigger the deployment of security

      affected population and responding agencies.                       or from the President; and

II. CONCEPT OF OPERATIONS                                          For the duration of the operations, the existing means of
                                                                   communications or whatever is applicable shall be utilized.
                                                                   However, the Emergency Telecommunication Cluster (ETC) will
The PNP as the lead agency in the Law and Order and Security       be activated once communication from the affected areas is cut.
Operations, together with members of the LAO Cluster such
as the AFP, BFP, BJMP, PCG, NBI, BuCor, and other agencies         B. Roles and Functions
that may be called upon by the PNP, will assist the response
operations by providing security assistance and other law and      The LAO Cluster shall consider the immediate establishment of 1)
order measures. Other agencies may be included based on their      communications through the ETC and 2) the Advance Command
law enforcement functions, as the situation warrants.              Post from the possible affected areas by the nearest territorial
                                                                   forces to stabilize security situation.
Cluster members shall maintain interoperability through their
established Operations Center (OPCEN). At the provincial
levels down to the municipalities and barangays, the leadership
in providing law and order shall be determined by the local
DRRMCs (LDRRMC) depending on which component of the
government is necessary.
